He doesn't throw it away. **End note**: Jazz thinks: *I don't know why I kept the form. I still have it. It's in my drawer. I look at it sometimes.* --- ## Chapter 6: The Launderette **Setting**: The launderette on the high street, Saturday afternoon. Jazz is there because his mum asked him to do the washing. Maya is there because she's been sleeping in the launderette. **Synopsis**: Jazz finds Maya asleep in the corner, behind the dryers. She wakes up when he drops a basket. She looks awful. She hasn't showered in days. She tells him she's been hiding. She tells him the Crows found her again. She tells him she doesn't know how much longer she can keep running. She asks him why he's still involved. He doesn't have an answer. He offers to buy her food. She says no. He buys her food anyway. They sit on the floor behind the dryers. The machines are loud and warm. She eats a pasty like she hasn't eaten in days. She probably hasn't. She asks him what he wants. He says he doesn't know. He says he thinks about the donor programme all the time. He says he doesn't understand why no one else seems to care. She laughs. It's not a nice laugh. She says, "They care. They just care more about the credits." She tells him she tried to tell people once. She wrote a letter to the local paper. It never got published. She went to the council. They told her the programme was voluntary. They told her she could stop anytime. They didn't mention what happens when you try. She says, "You can stop. But they make sure you wish you hadn't." She doesn't explain what that means. Jazz doesn't ask. He doesn't want to know. The dryers stop. The launderette is quiet. Maya stands up. She says she has to go. She says, "You should stop asking questions." She leaves. Jazz stays. He folds the washing. He goes home. **End note**: Jazz thinks: *I didn't stop asking questions. I wish I had. But I didn't.* --- ## Chapter 7: The Three of Them **Setting**: The school car park, after dark. Jazz is waiting for his mum to pick him up. She is late. He is alone. Then he is not alone. **Synopsis**: The Crows find him. There are three of them. They are wearing their black jumpers. They don't look threatening. They look tired. One of them — a girl with acne and thin hair — speaks. She says, "You've been talking to Maya." Jazz says he doesn't know what she's talking about. She says, "Don't lie. We've been watching you." She sounds sad, not angry. She says, "You need to stop." Jazz asks what happens if he doesn't. She doesn't answer. Another one — a boy with a cold — says, "She's not telling you the whole truth." He says, "Maya didn't stop donating because she got sick. She stopped because she got caught." Jazz asks what that means. The boy looks at the ground. The girl says, "There's a limit. If you go over the limit, the system flags you. And then they have to deal with you." She says, "Maya went over the limit. That's why she's running." Jazz asks what the limit is. No one answers. A car horn honks. His mum is here. The Crows step back. They let him go. He gets in the car. His mum asks if everything is okay. He says yes. He doesn't say anything else. He looks out the window. The Crows are standing under a lamppost. They are watching him leave. **End note**: Jazz thinks: *I didn't ask what happens if you go over the limit. I don't want to know. But I think about it all the time.* --- ## Chapter 8: The Jumpers **Setting**: Jazz's house. Sunday morning. He is in his room. He has made a decision. **Synopsis**: Jazz goes through his drawers. He finds the registration form from the community centre. He finds his phone. He finds a pair of scissors. He cuts the form into small pieces. He puts the pieces in the bin. He takes out his phone. He opens the thread with the unknown number. He reads the texts one more time. 🩸🩸🩸. Then he deletes the thread. He blocks the number. He puts his phone in his pocket. He goes downstairs. His mum is making tea. His sister is watching cartoons. Everything is normal. He sits down at the table. His mum asks if he wants toast. He says yes. He eats toast. He watches cartoons with his sister. He doesn't think about Maya. He doesn't think about the Crows. He doesn't think about the donor programme. He thinks about toast. He thinks about the weekend. He thinks about nothing. The chapter ends. Nothing happens. The story is over. **End note**: The last line is: *I ate my toast. It was fine. I didn't think about anything else. Not really. Not yet.*
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Character 1: Jazz Thorn **Background**: Fifteen, lives with his mum and younger sister in a small terraced house on the edge of Little Mithering. His dad left when he was seven — "went for milk," Jazz says flatly when anyone asks, which is the entire joke and he knows it. He's been the man of the house in theory only; in practice, he's just another person his mum has to look after. He's never had a proper friend group, just orbiters — kids who tolerate him because he's quiet and doesn't cause drama. He spends most of his time alone and has learned to prefer it. He doesn't realise this is a defence mechanism. He thinks he's just low-maintenance. **Personality, idiosyncracies, flaws**: Jazz is passive to the point of self-erasure. He watches things happen and does not intervene. He has strong opinions about everything but expresses none of them aloud. He keeps a mental list of "things I should have said" that he replays at night. He is observant in a way that would make him a good witness but a terrible friend — he notices when people are upset but doesn't know what to do with the information, so he does nothing. His main coping mechanism is understatement. When something bad happens, his internal response is "oh dear" or "that's not ideal." He is not brave. He is not kind in any active sense. He is not cruel either. He is just very, very good at not causing trouble. **Specific examples to ground him**: - He once watched a kid get his phone stolen on the bus and did nothing. He still thinks about it. He has never told anyone. - He has a box under his bed containing: a broken Nintendo DS, a fossil he found on a school trip, a Valentine's card he never sent (he doesn't remember who it was for), and a letter from his dad that he has never read all the way through. - He has never had a proper argument with anyone because he walks away before it escalates. - He knows the words to exactly three songs by heart and sings them in the shower badly. - He sometimes pretends to be asleep when his mum comes home late so he doesn't have to talk to her. **Physicality**: Tall for his age but slouches to compensate. Lanky, undefined, looks like he hasn't grown into his body yet. His school jumper is always slightly too big. He has dark circles under his eyes from poor sleep. He moves like he's trying not to take up space — shoulders curved in, hands in pockets, eyes on the ground. When he's nervous, he picks at the skin around his thumbs until they bleed. He doesn't make eye contact easily. When he talks, his voice is quiet and flat, like he's reading something he doesn't fully believe. **Motives**: Jazz doesn't know what he wants. That's the problem. He gets involved with Maya not because he's brave or noble but because he's curious and has nothing better to do. His main motive throughout the story is *not wanting to be involved* — he keeps trying to disengage, but he can't stop thinking about it. He is driven by guilt (for not helping the kid on the bus, for not reading his dad's letter, for all the small cowardices that make up his life) and a vague sense that he should be better than he is. He doesn't know how. **Allegiances**: He is loyal to his mum and sister by default, not by choice. He has no close friends. He feels a confusing sense of responsibility toward Maya that he can't articulate and doesn't act on. He does not trust the Crows. He does not trust Mr. Elmwood. He does not trust himself. **Relationships**: - **Mum**: They coexist. She works night shifts at a care home. She is tired. She loves him but doesn't know him. He loves her back in the same vague, undemonstrative way. They rarely talk about anything that matters. - **Sister (Ella, 8)**: He is fond of her in a distant, brotherly way. He helps with her homework sometimes. He doesn't protect her from anything real because he doesn't know how. - **Maya**: She confuses him. He doesn't know if he likes her or is afraid of her or both. He doesn't know why he keeps showing up. He doesn't know if he's helping her or using her as an excuse to feel less useless. **Flavour dialogue**: - *After Maya asks why he's still involved:* "I don't know. I keep thinking I'll figure it out and then I don't." - *Internal monologue, watching the Crows:* "They looked like anyone. That was the worst part. They could have been in my form class. They probably were." - *To his mum, when she asks if everything is okay:* "Yeah. Fine. Just tired." (He says this so often it has lost all meaning.) - *Internal monologue, after deleting the texts:* "I didn't feel better. I felt emptier. But maybe that was better. Maybe empty was okay." --- # Character 2: Maya Cross **Background**: Sixteen. She is from Little Mithering originally — born and raised. Her family still lives there, but she doesn't talk about them. She was a donor for two years before she stopped. She doesn't say exactly what happened when she stopped, but she flinches when people touch her unexpectedly and she sleeps with her back to the wall. She has been running for six months. She has stayed in three different towns. The Crows have found her every time. She doesn't know how. She is exhausted in a way that sleep cannot fix. **Personality, idiosyncracies, flaws**: Maya is brittle. She is sharp and defensive and quick to anger, but the anger is a front for something softer and more frightened. She trusts no one and is desperate for someone to prove her wrong. She is bad at accepting help — she either rejects it outright or clings too hard, with no middle ground. She has a dark sense of humour that surfaces at inappropriate moments, which makes people think she's handling things better than she is. She is not handling things. She is surviving, which is not the same thing. She lies easily and often, not because she's malicious but because the truth is too heavy to carry alone. She has told Jazz approximately three versions of her story. None of them are entirely true. None of them are entirely false. **Specific examples to ground her**: - She can't eat in front of people. She doesn't know why. She just can't. - She counts her steps obsessively — not because she's superstitious, but because it's something to focus on when her brain is spiralling. - She has a photograph of her younger brother in her coat pocket. She doesn't look at it. She just needs it to be there. - She has never owned a phone that wasn't prepaid or stolen. She changes numbers every few weeks. - She bites her nails so badly they bleed. She doesn't notice she's doing it. **Physicality**: Small and wiry, built for hiding. She moves like she's always looking for an exit — even when sitting still, her eyes track the room, her weight shifts, her hands are never fully relaxed. She has a habit of touching her inner elbow where the donation scars are, a repetitive, unconscious gesture. Her hair is scraped back aggressively, like she doesn't want anything touching her face. She wears layers, always, even when it's warm. She looks younger than she is, which she uses to her advantage. When she's scared, her voice goes very quiet and very flat. When she's angry, she goes still. **Motives**: Maya wants two things: to survive, and to understand what happened to her. She doesn't know if she wants to expose the Initiative or just escape it. She doesn't know if she wants justice or just rest. She is driven by fear and a stubborn, fading hope that things could be different. She keeps running because stopping means accepting that this is all there is. **Allegiances**: She is loyal to no one. She has learned that attachment is a liability. She feels a protective instinct toward Jazz that frustrates her — she didn't want him involved, and she resents him for making her care. She is not loyal to the Initiative, obviously, but she is still shaped by it. She follows its rules even when she's running. She doesn't know how not to. **Relationships**: - **Family**: She doesn't talk about them. Jazz doesn't ask. The photograph in her pocket is a clue he hasn't pushed. - **Jazz**: She sees him as a liability and a lifeline. She keeps pushing him away and keeps coming back. She doesn't know what she wants from him. She doesn't know if she wants anything. She just knows she's less alone when he's there, and that terrifies her. - **The Crows**: She knew them before. She went to school with them. They were normal kids once. She doesn't know if she hates them or pities them. Maybe both. **Flavour dialogue**: - *To Jazz, when he asks what happens if the Crows catch her:* "They don't catch you. They find you. And then you go back. And then you don't leave again." (She doesn't say what "back" means. She doesn't have to.) - *Internal monologue, watching Jazz eat:* "He chewed with his mouth open. It was disgusting. I wanted to tell him to stop. I didn't. I liked watching him eat. It meant he was normal. It meant something could be normal." - *When Jazz offers to help:* "I didn't ask you to help me. I didn't ask you for anything. So stop looking at me like I'm your project." - *Internal monologue, alone:* "I don't know where my brother is. I don't know if he's okay. I don't know if he's still donating. I don't know if he's still alive. I don't know why I keep the photograph. I don't know why I don't look at it. I don't know anything." --- # Character 3: Lauren (Crow #1) **Background**: Fifteen. She has been a Crow for two years. She joined because her mum was sick and the Initiative paid for her treatment. She doesn't talk about this. She doesn't think about what she does as bad — she thinks of it as monitoring. Keeping the system running. Keeping people safe. She genuinely believes this, most of the time. She has bad days where she doesn't. On those days, she doesn't sleep. **Personality, idiosyncracies, flaws**: Lauren is the one who speaks. She has appointed herself the group's spokesperson because she is the best at sounding reasonable. She is not cruel, but she is efficient. She does what she is told because asking questions would mean admitting there are questions to ask. She is deeply uncomfortable with Jazz because he reminds her of herself before she joined — the mild curiosity, the reluctance to let things go. She sees herself in him and doesn't like it. She has acne that she picks at when she's stressed. She wears her black jumper even in summer because it makes her feel like part of something. She doesn't know what she would be without it. **Physicality**: Average height, average build, unremarkable. That's the point. She is designed to be forgettable. She stands with her hands clasped behind her back, like a soldier at ease. She makes eye contact when she speaks, which is more unsettling than if she didn't. She has a calm, measured voice that never wavers. She looks like she could be anyone's daughter, which she is. **Motives**: Lauren wants to believe she is a good person. She wants the system to be fair. She wants her mum to stay healthy. She wants to not have to think about the moral implications of her job. She wants to be proud of herself. **Allegiances**: She is loyal to the Initiative because the Initiative saved her mother. She is loyal to the other Crows because they are the only people who understand. She is not loyal to Jazz or Maya, but she feels a twinge of something when she sees them — guilt, maybe. She doesn't examine it. **Flavour dialogue**: - *To Jazz, in the car park:* "I'm not going to hurt you. I'm just going to tell you something, and then I'm going to leave. What you do with it is your business." - *To Maya, once, before everything changed:* "I don't like this either. But my mum is alive. And I'd do it again. I would." - *Internal monologue, after Jazz leaves:* "He's going to keep digging. I can see it in his face. He's going to find something. And I don't know if I'm going to stop him or help him." --- # Character 4: Mr. Elmwood **Background**: Late forties. Biology teacher. Has been at Little Mithering Comprehensive for eighteen years. He is well-liked, considered a "character" — he wears novelty ties, he does magic tricks at the end of term, he remembers everyone's birthdays. He runs the Donation Initiative's local branch. He took over from his predecessor twelve years ago. He has never questioned the programme. He genuinely, wholeheartedly believes it is a force for good. This is not an act. This is the most frightening thing about him. **Personality, idiosyncracies, flaws**: Mr. Elmwood is warm, friendly, and utterly unreflective. He has a jovial, slightly bumbling manner that disarms people. He calls everyone "champ" and "sport." He laughs easily. He deflects difficult questions with gentle humour. He is not stupid, but he has built his entire identity around not looking too closely at things that might upset him. He knows, on some level, that the programme has costs. He does not think about them. If pressed, he would say that the ends justify the means. He would say it with a sad smile, like he was explaining a difficult but necessary truth. He would mean it. **Specific examples to ground him**: - He keeps a tin of mints on his desk and offers them to students who look upset. He does not ask why they are upset. - He has a photograph of his own daughter on his desk. She is a donor. He is very proud of her. - He cries at the end of every school year during the leavers' assembly. He cries at sentimental adverts too. - He has never read a news article critically in his life. He reads the headlines and moves on. - He believes, sincerely, that he is one of the good ones. **Physicality**: Round, soft, comfortably middle-aged. Bald on top, with the remaining hair trimmed short. He wears glasses that he pushes up his nose constantly. He has a warm, booming voice that carries across a room. He gestures expansively when he talks. He claps people on the shoulder. He fills a room without meaning to. He is impossible to dislike and therefore impossible to confront. **Motives**: Mr. Elmwood wants to help people. He wants to be appreciated. He wants the programme to continue because the programme is his life's work. He wants to retire knowing he made a difference. He does not want to think about the donors who couldn't handle it. He does not want to think about Maya. She was one of his best students. He doesn't know what happened to her. He doesn't want to know. **Allegiances**: He is loyal to the Initiative. He is loyal to the town. He is loyal to the idea that he is a good man. These things are in order of priority. **Flavour dialogue**: - *At the information session:* "It's a beautiful thing, really. A community coming together to support its own. We all give a little. We all get a little. That's the deal. That's what makes this town special." - *To Jazz, one-on-one:* "You're a bright lad, Jasper. I've always thought so. You ask good questions. That's important. But sometimes the best questions are the ones we don't need to ask. Sometimes we just need to trust that people know what they're doing." - *Internal monologue, alone in his office at night:* "I hope she's okay. I hope she's not angry. I hope she understands. I did what I had to do. I did what was best for everyone." (He almost believes it.) --- # Character 5: Ella Thorn **Background**: Eight years old. Jazz's little sister. She is in Year 4. She is unaware of almost everything happening in the story. She is the normal one. She is the reason Jazz has to keep pretending things are fine. **Personality**: She is loud, demanding, and genuinely delightful. She has no filter. She tells strangers their hair looks nice. She cries when she drops her toast. She is obsessed with horses and has never been near one. She thinks her big brother is the funniest person alive. She doesn't know he's sad. She doesn't know anything is wrong. This is a gift and a burden. **Physicality**: Small, perpetually sticky, hair always escaping its ponytail. She runs everywhere. She talks with her hands. She climbs on furniture. She is a hurricane in a small body. **Motives**: She wants a horse. She wants her brother to play with her. She wants her mum to not be so tired. She wants to be older. She doesn't understand why that's a bad thing to wish for. **Flavour dialogue**: - *To Jazz, while he's staring into space:* "What are you looking at? Is there a spider? Can I see? Let me see. Jazz. JAZZ." - *At dinner, innocently:* "Mum, why do the nice men in black jumpers come to our school? They're scary. I don't like them." (She doesn't know she's said something important. Jazz and his mother exchange a look. Nothing is said.)

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: The Wrong Number The text came just after midnight. I was awake because I was always awake. That’s not something I’m proud of. It’s just what happens when you share a wall with your mum’s bedroom and she works nights and you’ve trained your brain to listen for her coming home. I wasn’t waiting for her. I was just waiting. There’s a difference. My phone buzzed on the floor next to my charger. I picked it up. The screen was too bright. I squinted. 🩸🩸🩸 No sender name. No context. Just three little red droplets glowing at me in the dark. I stared at it for a while. Long enough that the screen dimmed and I had to tap it to wake it up again. I checked the number. It wasn’t in my contacts. It wasn’t a local code I recognised. I thought about replying. I thought about screenshotting it. I thought about showing it to someone. But it was midnight and my mum was at work and my sister was asleep and there was no one to show it to anyway. I put the phone down. I picked it up again. I googled “blood emoji meaning” and got a list of articles about period tracking apps and vampire aesthetics. Nothing helpful. I googled “three blood drops text” and got a Wikipedia page about blood types. I closed the browser. I felt stupid for being weirded out. I texted back: who is this Nothing came back. I waited five minutes. Ten. I fell asleep with the phone in my hand. — In the morning, the text was still there. I looked at it while I was getting dressed. I looked at it while I brushed my teeth. I looked at it at breakfast. “You’re quiet,” my mum said. She was eating a piece of toast over the sink, which was how she ate most things. She had her work coat on already, even though she’d just got home. She would sleep for a few hours and then do it all again. “Just tired.” “You should go to bed earlier.” “Yeah.” This was our entire conversation. It was the same one we had almost every morning. I finished my cereal. My sister Ella came down in her school jumper backwards and demanded I fix it. I fixed it. We left. — School was school. Nothing happened. I forgot about the text for most of the day. It sat in my pocket like a coin I didn’t remember putting there. I almost deleted it at lunch. I didn’t. I thought about it again on the bus home. Just a flicker. Three red drops. Probably a wrong number. Probably a group chat I’d been added to by accident. Probably nothing. I was good at convincing myself things were nothing. — That night, I checked my phone before bed. No new messages. I opened the thread again. The three emojis. My unanswered reply. I blocked the number. Then I unblocked it. Then I left my phone on the floor and tried to sleep. I didn’t sleep well. — The second text came three nights later. Same number. Same three drops. I was awake again. I watched it arrive. The phone buzzed once and lit up the ceiling. I picked it up. My hands were cold. I typed: why are you sending me this A pause. Then three dots. Then a reply. you know what it means I didn’t. I really didn’t. But something in my chest went tight anyway. I called the number before I could think about it. It rang. Someone picked up. There was breathing. Not heavy. Just there. A presence on the other end of the line. Then a voice. Older. A woman or a man, I couldn’t tell. Just a voice that said: “You shouldn’t have called this number.” The line went dead. I sat in the dark holding my phone. My heart was doing something I didn’t like. I put the phone down. I picked it up again. I put it under my pillow like that would help. I didn’t sleep. — The next morning, I found her. She was sitting on the steps outside the art block, eating a packet of cheap biscuits like she hadn’t had a proper meal in days. She was new. I’d seen her in registration but we hadn’t spoken. She had a plaster on her inner elbow, half-hidden by her sleeve. I didn’t plan to talk to her. I didn’t plan anything. I just walked over and stood there like an idiot until she looked up. “You got the text,” she said. Not a question. I opened my mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. “How do you know?” She finished her biscuit. Brushed the crumbs off her lap. Looked at me with eyes that were too old for her face. “Because I sent it.” She stood up. She was shorter than me. She looked at me like she was deciding something. “I send it to everyone,” she said. “Everyone who’s new. Everyone who might not know yet. You’re supposed to ignore it. Most people do.” “Know what?” She didn’t answer. She looked over my shoulder. I turned. Three kids in black jumpers were standing at the edge of the playground, not looking at us, but positioned so they could see everything. “Don’t follow me,” she said. She walked away. The kids in black watched her go. One of them wrote something in a small notebook. I stood there. The bell rang. I didn’t move. My phone was in my pocket. The text was still there. Three red drops. I hadn’t deleted it. I didn’t know why. I still don’t.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and a strong command of the voice established in the writing plan. The first-person narration from Jazz is convincingly rendered — flat, understated, self-aware without being precocious. The voice feels genuinely teenage without resorting to slang-heavy affectation or the kind of performed authenticity that often plagues YA fiction. The structural choice to compress the timeline is interesting. The plan called for Chapter 1 to cover only the first text and the morning after, with Maya's introduction saved for Chapter 2. Instead, the model has folded the second text and the first encounter with Maya into Chapter 1, effectively merging what was planned as Chapters 1-3 into a single chapter. This is a significant deviation from the plan, though it's not necessarily a failure — the pacing is tighter for it, and the chapter doesn't feel overstuffed. However, it does mean the chapter loses some of the planned "anticlimax" quality — the slow burn of Jazz forgetting about the text, the mundane morning, the cereal. Those elements are present but compressed. The dialogue is sparse and effective. "You got the text" as a non-question is a strong choice. Maya's explanation — "I send it to everyone. Everyone who's new. Everyone who might not know yet. You're supposed to ignore it. Most people do." — is economical and creates genuine unease without over-explaining. The voice on the phone ("You shouldn't have called this number") is appropriately chilling without being melodramatic. The characterisation of Jazz is consistent with his profile: passive, observant, unable to act decisively, fixating on small details (the plaster, the biscuits). The relationship with his mother is rendered in exactly the right register — two people coexisting, the same conversation every morning. Ella appears briefly and naturally. The prose avoids purple passages almost entirely. There's one slightly weak moment — "eyes that were too old for her face" — which is a cliché, but it's the only real slip. The line "My heart was doing something I didn't like" is a good example of the planned understatement working well. The ending is strong. The three kids in black, the notebook, the bell, Jazz standing still — it's appropriately anticlimactic while still being unsettling. The final lines ("I hadn't deleted it. I didn't know why. I still don't.") echo the planned voice effectively. The comedy is present but subtle — the Google search results, the blocking and unblocking of the number, the "I felt stupid for being weirded out." These land without being laboured. The main weakness is the deviation from the chapter plan. The plan explicitly called for Maya's introduction in Chapter 2, with Chapter 1 ending at breakfast with Jazz going to school. By compressing the timeline, the model has front-loaded the mystery and lost some of the planned mundane texture. The chapter also introduces the phone call (planned for Chapter 3) and the Crows (planned for Chapter 2), which means subsequent chapters will need to find new material or risk repetition. The "wrong number" premise — central to the story's concept — is not yet established. In the plan, Jazz is supposed to be a wrong number. This chapter doesn't address that, which is fine for Chapter 1, but the compression means the reader doesn't get the slow build of Jazz's ordinary life before the disruption. Overall, this is a confident, well-executed piece of YA fiction with a distinctive voice, effective restraint, and genuine atmosphere. The deviations from the plan are the primary concern, but the writing itself is of high quality. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: The New Girl and the Plaster Her name was Maya Cross. I found that out because I asked someone in my form class, and they said she’d transferred in from somewhere up north, and that was all anyone knew. No one had talked to her. No one was going to. That’s how it works in a small school. New people are interesting for about a day, and then they’re just the person who sits at the back. I watched her all morning. I don’t know why. She didn’t do anything interesting. She took notes. She didn’t put her hand up. She ate her lunch alone behind the PE shed. She had the same plaster on her arm, and when she thought no one was looking, she touched it. Just a quick press of her fingers, like checking a wound was still there. The kids in black jumpers watched her too. Not all the time. Just enough. I saw one of them — a girl with thin hair and acne — standing at the end of the corridor during break, not looking at Maya but positioned so Maya couldn’t get past without passing her. I saw another one, a boy with a cold, sitting on the benches near the PE shed while Maya ate her lunch. He wasn’t reading. He wasn’t on his phone. He was just sitting. I didn’t know what to do with this information. I kept it in my head like a puzzle I wasn’t sure I wanted to solve. — After school, I found her again. She was walking toward the canal path, fast, with her hood up. I followed her. I didn’t know what I was going to say. I just knew I had to say something. “Hey.” She didn’t stop. “Hey. Maya.” She stopped. She turned. Her face was tired and sharp and she looked like she hadn’t slept in a week. “What.” “You sent me the text. You said you send it to everyone. What does it mean?” She looked at me for a long time. Then she looked past me, at the road behind. Then she looked at me again. “You really want to know?” “Yes.” “No you don’t.” “I do.” She laughed. It wasn’t a nice laugh. It was short and tired and it ended quickly. “Fine. Meet me at the canal after school tomorrow. Don’t tell anyone. Don’t bring anyone. And if anyone asks where you’re going, lie.” She walked away. I watched her go. I stood there until she was a small dot in the distance, and then I stood there a bit longer. — I didn’t tell my mum. I didn’t tell anyone. I went home. I did my homework. I ate dinner. I watched telly with Ella. I went to bed. I lay awake staring at the ceiling. I kept thinking about the voice on the phone. *You shouldn’t have called this number.* I kept thinking about the kids in black. I kept thinking about the plaster on Maya’s arm and the way she touched it, like she was checking it was still there. I didn’t sleep well. — The next day was cold and grey and the heating was broken and everyone was in a bad mood. I didn’t care. I spent the whole day waiting for the final bell. At four o’clock, I walked to the canal. She was already there. Sitting on a bench near the lock, looking at the water. She didn’t turn around when I sat down next to her. “You came,” she said. “Yeah.” “You shouldn’t have.” “Probably not.” She was quiet for a while. The canal was brown and still. A plastic bottle floated past. I watched it go. “I was a donor,” she said finally. I waited. “The Donation Initiative. It’s a programme. Teenagers give blood. The town uses it for stuff — healthcare, school funding, all that. Everyone knows about it. It’s not a secret. It’s just... not talked about.” She pulled up her sleeve. There were scars on her inner elbow. Small, white, circular. Dozens of them. “I started when I was fourteen. My mum lost her job. My dad wasn’t around. The credits covered our rent for six months. It was fine. It was fine for a long time.” She stopped. She pulled her sleeve back down. “Then it wasn’t fine.” I waited for her to continue. She didn’t. “What happened?” I asked. She shook her head. “I stopped. And when you stop, they don’t like it. They find you. They bring you back. They make sure you don’t leave again.” “Who?” She looked at me. Her eyes were very clear and very empty. “The kids in the black jumpers. They’re not just kids. They’re trackers. They’re the ones who make sure you stay in the system. They found me twice already. They’ll find me again.” I thought about the girl with acne. The boy with the cold. They looked like anyone. They looked like they could be in my form class. “Why do you send the texts?” She looked away. “Because someone sent them to me. Before I was a donor. Before I knew what it meant. A warning. I didn’t understand it then. I want other people to have the chance to understand.” “A warning for what?” She stood up. “For what happens if you sign up. For what happens if you don’t. For all of it.” She looked down at me. The light was going. The canal was dark. Her face was hard to read. “Delete the number. Forget you met me. Go home.” She walked away. I stayed on the bench. The water moved slowly. A swan drifted past, white and silent. I didn’t delete the number. I don’t know why. I still don’t.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a competent but somewhat compressed execution of the plan. The model has merged what was originally planned as two separate chapters (Chapter 2: The New Girl and the Plaster, and Chapter 4: The Canal) into a single chapter, which means the slow burn of Maya's introduction and the separate canal meeting are collapsed together. This is a significant deviation from the chapter plan, which called for Maya to approach Jazz at lunch with the cryptic "You got a weird text last night" line, and for the canal meeting to happen in Chapter 4. Instead, Jazz follows Maya after school and the canal conversation happens immediately. This compression loses some of the planned tension and pacing. That said, the writing itself is largely effective. Jazz's voice is consistent with the profile: flat, understated, observational without being poetic. The prose avoids purple flourishes almost entirely. The line "I kept it in my head like a puzzle I wasn't sure I wanted to solve" is the closest thing to a metaphor, and it's functional rather than strained. The swan at the end ("white and silent") is the one moment that edges toward the symbolic, but the plan explicitly warned against this, and the model includes it anyway. It's minor but worth noting. The dialogue is mostly strong. Maya's lines feel authentic to her character profile - brittle, defensive, giving information in fragments. "No you don't" / "I do" is a good exchange. The revelation of the scars is handled with appropriate restraint. However, the exposition in the canal scene is somewhat more direct than the plan intended - Maya explains the system fairly clearly, whereas the plan called for her to withhold information and leave things fragmentary. The plan said "She doesn't explain who 'they' are. She doesn't explain why the texts are sent." In the chapter, she actually does explain quite a bit, including the purpose of the texts as warnings. This makes Maya slightly less mysterious and the world-building slightly more tidy than intended. The Crows are introduced effectively - the girl with acne and the boy with the cold are observed with the right kind of flat specificity. The detail of the boy "not reading, not on his phone, just sitting" is good showing rather than telling. The chapter's structure is slightly awkward - the section breaks feel somewhat arbitrary, and the chapter has a slightly rushed quality because it's doing the work of two planned chapters. The ending with "I don't know why. I still don't" is consistent with Jazz's voice and the plan's instruction for flat, exhausted endings. The plaster detail from the plan is used well - the scars revealed later pay it off. However, the plan's specific lunch scene ("Maya sits near him. Not with him. Near him.") is entirely absent, replaced by Jazz following her after school. This loses some of the social texture the plan was building. Characters are consistent with their profiles. Jazz is passive, observational, unable to disengage. Maya is brittle, gives information in fragments, pushes him away. The Crows are observed as ordinary-looking kids, which is correct. The comedy is largely absent from this chapter - the plan called for Jazz's inappropriate internal reactions and dark humor, but this chapter is mostly straight-faced. The plan's voice notes emphasized Jazz's flat, sometimes funny internal monologue, and that's somewhat muted here. Overall: competent, faithful to the general spirit, but compressed in ways that sacrifice some of the planned texture, and slightly more expository than intended. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 12 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Second Text I didn't see Maya for three days. She wasn't at school. Her seat in registration was empty. I asked someone — a girl in my form who seemed to know things — and she said Maya had been pulled out of classes for a "medical assessment." That was the phrase. Medical assessment. I didn't believe it for a second. I thought about going to the canal. I thought about looking for her. I didn't do either. I went to school. I came home. I did my homework. I watched telly with Ella. I lay awake at night staring at the ceiling. The text was still on my phone. I hadn't deleted it. I opened it sometimes, just to look at the three red drops, like they might mean something different than they did the last time. They never did. — On the third night, my phone buzzed again. Same number. Same three emojis. But this time there was something else. A message underneath. *you know what it means* My chest went tight. I typed back before I could stop myself. i don't Three dots appeared. Then disappeared. Then appeared again. *you will* I waited for more. Nothing came. I typed again. what do you want from me A long pause. Then: *we don't want anything from you. we want you to stay out of it. you're not supposed to be involved.* I stared at the screen. My hands were cold. I typed: who is this The three dots appeared. Disappeared. Then the message came through, and it wasn't what I expected. *someone who used to be in your position. someone who made the wrong choice. delete the number, jazz. forget about her. forget about all of it. it's not too late.* I stared at my name. They knew my name. I hadn't told them my name. I hadn't told Maya my name. I typed: who are you No reply. I called the number. It rang once and went to voicemail. A generic voicemail. No name. No greeting. Just a beep. I hung up. I sat in the dark with my phone in my hand. My heart was doing that thing again. The thing I didn't like. — The next morning, I found one of them. The girl with acne. The one who'd been watching Maya in the corridor. She was standing near the bike sheds, alone, drinking from a water bottle. She saw me coming and didn't move. "You're Jazz," she said. "How do you know my name?" She shrugged. "I know a lot of things." I stepped closer. She didn't step back. "Where is Maya?" "She's not here." "I know she's not here. Where is she?" The girl looked at me for a long moment. Then she said something I didn't expect. "She's safe. For now. But she won't be if you keep asking questions." "What does that mean?" She sighed. She looked tired. She looked younger than I'd thought. Maybe fourteen. Maybe fifteen. Her acne was bad, red and angry along her jawline. She picked at it when she talked. "It means you need to stop. You're not a donor. You're not on the list. You're not anything. You got a wrong number and you made it into something it's not. Let it go." "Why do you care?" She didn't answer. She finished her water. Put the bottle in her bag. "Because I don't want you to end up like her." She walked away. I watched her go. She didn't look back. — I didn't let it go. I went to the library at lunch. I used the school computers. I searched for "Little Mithering Donation Initiative." The first result was a council page from 2001, full of jargon about "community health optimisation" and "intergenerational resource sharing." It sounded like it was written by someone who'd been paid per word. I scrolled through it. There were graphs. There were testimonials from parents. There was a photo of a smiling teenager with a plaster on her arm and the caption: *"Giving back feels good."* I searched for anything negative. I found nothing. No news articles. No blog posts. No forum threads. Just the same official page, repeated across multiple sites. Like someone had cleaned up the internet and forgotten to leave any mess. I searched for Maya Cross. Nothing. I searched for "blood donor programme teens UK." I got a page about the NHS blood service. I closed the browser. I sat in the library and stared at the screen. The librarian asked if I was okay. I said yes. She didn't believe me. I didn't care. — That night, I sent one more message. i'm not going to stop I waited. No reply. I waited longer. Nothing. I put my phone on the floor. I lay on my back. I stared at the ceiling. My phone buzzed. I picked it up. *then you've already made your choice.* The number went dead after that. I tried calling. Nothing. I tried texting. Nothing. The number was gone. Disconnected. Like it had never existed. I sat in the dark. My phone was warm in my hand. I thought about Maya. I thought about the girl with acne. I thought about the voice on the phone, the one that told me I shouldn't have called. I thought about the three red drops. Still there. Still at the top of my messages. The very first thing I saw when I opened my phone. I didn't delete them. I don't know why. I still don't.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this piece demonstrates a strong command of the established voice and tone. Jazz's flat, understated narration is consistent and effective — the deadpan interiority ("My heart was doing that thing again. The thing I didn't like") lands as intended, capturing a teenager who processes fear through deliberate understatement rather than melodrama. The voice is genuinely distinctive and not merely performed. The chapter follows its plan closely. The second text arrives, Jazz calls the number, someone warns him off, he finds Maya (or rather, finds a Crow), he investigates at the library, and the chapter ends with him refusing to delete the number. The plan called for Maya to appear and tell him to meet at the canal — this is notably absent. Instead, the chapter substitutes an encounter with Lauren (the Crow) and ends without the canal meeting being arranged. This is a meaningful deviation: the canal scene is pushed to Chapter 4, but the chapter plan for Chapter 3 explicitly included Maya's appearance and the canal invitation. The model has restructured the chapter, which is defensible creatively but represents a departure from the plan. The library scene is well-handled. The detail of the council page from 2001 with its bureaucratic language ("community health optimisation," "intergenerational resource sharing") is exactly the right kind of horror — mundane, institutional, scrubbed clean. The absence of any negative search results is more chilling than any explicit threat would be. This is the chapter's strongest moment. The dialogue is functional and mostly earns its place. Lauren's lines ("Because I don't want you to end up like her") are appropriately understated. The text exchange is well-paced. However, the mysterious texter's messages occasionally tip into slightly generic thriller territory — "you've already made your choice" is a line that could appear in any number of YA mysteries. It's not bad, but it's the chapter's weakest moment in terms of originality. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are no strained metaphors, no overwrought descriptions. The prose is clean and controlled. The one moment that risks sentimentality — "Like it had never existed" — is brief and earns its place. Character consistency is strong. Jazz's passivity-that-looks-like-stubbornness is well-rendered: he doesn't act heroically, he just can't stop. The Crow (Lauren) is appropriately ambiguous — tired, not threatening, picking at her acne. This grounds her as a real person rather than a plot device. The chapter is emotionally engaging in a quiet, accumulative way. It doesn't reach for big moments. The horror is in the gaps — what the texter knows, what the library doesn't show, what Lauren won't say. This is the right approach for this story. One weakness: the chapter is slightly repetitive in structure. Jazz receives information, doesn't act, receives more information, doesn't act. This is intentional (Jazz's passivity is the point) but the rhythm becomes slightly predictable by the third iteration. The plan's note about "jagged" pacing isn't fully realized here — the chapter moves in a fairly straight line despite its circular content. The ending is appropriately flat and earns its place. "I don't know why. I still don't." is exactly the kind of non-resolution the writing plan called for. Overall, this is a competent, often impressive chapter that executes its brief well. It's not a masterpiece — the dialogue occasionally defaults to genre convention, the structural deviation from the plan is notable, and the pacing is slightly more linear than intended — but it demonstrates genuine craft and a secure grip on voice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: The Canal She was back. I saw her first thing Monday morning, standing at the school gates like she'd never been gone. Same hoodie. Same tired eyes. Same plaster on her arm. She looked at me once, briefly, and then looked away. I got the message. But I didn't stay away. I found her at lunch, behind the PE shed, eating another packet of cheap biscuits. She saw me coming and sighed. "You didn't delete the number." "No." "I told you to." "I know." She broke a biscuit in half. Ate one piece. Stared at the other. "They took my phone," she said. "When they found me. They took it and they sent messages from it. To you. They wanted to see if you'd keep replying." I felt cold. "They said you didn't stop. They said you kept pushing. They told me I had to deal with it or they would." "Deal with it how?" She didn't answer. She ate the other half of the biscuit. "Maya. Deal with it how?" She looked at me. Her eyes were red at the edges. "I'm supposed to convince you to stop. If I can't, they'll do it themselves." I sat down next to her. The ground was damp. I didn't care. "So convince me." She laughed. That same short, tired laugh. "I can't. You're stubborn. You're stupid. You're not going to stop." "Then why are you here?" She was quiet for a long time. A crow landed on the fence nearby. It watched us with its head tilted. "Because I don't want to be alone," she said. "Is that enough?" I didn't know if it was. I said yes anyway. — We sat behind the PE shed until the bell rang. She told me things. Not everything. Just enough. She told me she'd been on the run for six months. She told me she'd stayed in three different towns, worked cash-in-hand jobs, slept in launderettes and bus stations and once in a church. She told me the Crows had found her every time. She didn't know how. She thought maybe they had something in her blood. A marker. A tracker. She didn't know if that was possible. She didn't know anything anymore. She told me about her little brother. "He's eight," she said. "Same age as your sister. He's still a donor. They started him last year." I felt something cold settle in my stomach. "He's still in town. He lives with my mum. She doesn't know I'm gone. She thinks I'm at a boarding school. That's what they told her. That's what they tell all the parents." "Does she believe it?" Maya shrugged. "She wants to believe it. It's easier." The bell rang. She stood up. Brushed crumbs off her lap. "Meet me at the canal tonight. After dark. There's something I want to show you." — I went. I told my mum I was going to a friend's house. She didn't ask which friend. She didn't ask anything. She just said be back by ten and went back to her crossword. The canal was dark and quiet. The water was black. The only light came from the houses on the far bank, small and distant. Maya was already there, sitting on the bench near the lock. She had a torch. She shone it at the water. "Down there," she said. "Look." I looked. I didn't see anything at first. Just dark water. Then the torch beam caught something. A shape. A box. A metal crate, half-submerged, chained to the bank. "What is that?" She didn't answer. She walked along the bank to where the chain was padlocked to a metal ring. She pulled a key from her pocket. Unlocked it. "You knew about this?" "I found it months ago. Before I left. I never told anyone." She pulled the chain. The crate shifted. Water sloshed. Something inside it clinked. I helped her pull it up. It was heavy. We dragged it onto the bank. The metal was rusted. The lid was sealed with a plastic tie. Maya cut the tie with a small knife. She lifted the lid. Inside were files. Dozens of them. Paper, mostly. Some in plastic sleeves. Some water-damaged. Some so old the ink had blurred. "What are these?" "Records," she said. "Donor records. Going back to the 1980s. Names. Ages. Donation schedules. Yields. Complications. Deaths." She picked one up. Handed it to me. The paper was thin and yellowed. I opened it. A name. A date of birth. A list of donations. Every two weeks. Then every week. Then every three days. Then a stamp: *DECEASED — SYSTEM ERROR.* Below it, in handwriting: *No error. Protocol followed.* I looked at Maya. Her face was pale in the torchlight. "There are hundreds of them," she said. "I've been reading them for months. Trying to find a pattern. Trying to find proof." "Proof of what?" She looked at me. The torchlight made her eyes look hollow. "Proof that the programme kills people. And that everyone in this town knows it."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a significant departure from the writing plan in ways that are both interesting and problematic. Let me work through the key elements. ADHERENCE TO PLAN: The chapter plan specified a canal scene where Maya tells Jazz the truth "but not all of it" — she explains the donor programme, the credits system, the Crows as monitors, and ends with her walking away while Jazz sits on the bench. Instead, the model has written a substantially different chapter: Maya has been caught and returned, the Crows have used her phone to test Jazz, and the chapter culminates in a dramatic discovery of a submerged metal crate full of donor records going back to the 1980s, including files stamped "DECEASED — SYSTEM ERROR." This is a major deviation. The plan explicitly warned against exposition dumps and dramatic revelations. The crate of files is precisely the kind of dramatic plot device the plan was trying to avoid — it's a thriller trope, not the quiet, fragmented, uncertain horror the plan was building toward. The plan also specified that Maya "doesn't explain who 'they' are" and "doesn't say what happens when they do" — the chapter here is actually more restrained in some ways but introduces a much bigger revelation than planned. VOICE AND TONE: The Jazz voice is reasonably well-maintained. The flat, understated first-person narration works. Lines like "I sat down next to her. The ground was damp. I didn't care." capture the intended register. The dialogue is spare and functional. However, the chapter loses the darkly comic element almost entirely — there's no inappropriate humour, no Jazz fixating on stupid details, no wrong-moment levity. The plan called for comedy from Jazz's internal monologue, and it's largely absent here. THE CRATE PROBLEM: The metal crate is a serious tonal misstep. It's a thriller device — the hidden evidence, the dramatic reveal, the files with "DECEASED — SYSTEM ERROR" and handwritten "No error. Protocol followed." This is exactly the kind of clean, dramatic revelation the plan was trying to avoid. The plan said "information comes in fragments, some of it wrong, some of it contradictory" — instead we get a neat box of proof. The stamp "DECEASED — SYSTEM ERROR" followed by handwritten "No error. Protocol followed" is actually quite effective as a horror detail, but it belongs to a different, more conventional story than the one being planned. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly functional and appropriately spare. "Because I don't want to be alone. Is that enough?" is a good line. The exchange about convincing Jazz to stop is natural. However, some of the exposition dialogue is clunky — Maya explaining the crate's contents reads like an info-dump: "Records. Donor records. Going back to the 1980s. Names. Ages. Donation schedules. Yields. Complications. Deaths." This is exactly the kind of exposition the plan warned against. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Maya is reasonably consistent — scared, brittle, trusting Jazz too quickly because she's desperate. The detail about her brother being eight, same age as Ella, is effective and earned. Jazz's passivity and stubbornness are present. However, Maya having a key to a padlocked crate she's known about for months but never acted on strains credibility. THE CROW DETAIL: The crow landing on the fence and watching them "with its head tilted" is a moment the plan explicitly warned against — "The crow at the end is just a bird. Let things be what they are." Here it's used as atmospheric punctuation, which is exactly the kind of easy symbolism the plan rejected. PROSE QUALITY: The prose is clean and controlled. No purple prose. No forced metaphors. The physical details work — the damp ground, the black water, the rusted crate. The torchlight making Maya's eyes look hollow is a slight reach but not egregious. ENDING: The chapter ends on a dramatic revelation rather than an anticlimax, which directly contradicts the plan's instruction that "some chapters should just stop" and "let the reader feel the anticlimax." OVERALL: A competent chapter that maintains the voice reasonably well but fundamentally misunderstands the story's intended approach to horror and revelation. The crate is a thriller device in a story that was supposed to resist thriller devices. The chapter is readable and has good moments, but it betrays the plan's most important structural and tonal commitments. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 10 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 12 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 8 Characters Consistent with Profile: 12 Followed Chapter Plan: 5 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 12

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: The Community Centre I didn't sleep that night. I lay in bed with the image of the crate in my head. The rusted metal. The waterlogged files. The handwriting on the death records. *No error. Protocol followed.* I kept seeing it every time I closed my eyes. The next day, I found a flyer in the school office. Tucked behind the reception desk, next to a stack of permission slips. *The Donation Initiative: Information Session. All welcome. Refreshments provided.* I took one. I folded it and put it in my pocket. I didn't tell Maya. The session was on Thursday evening, at the community centre on Mill Road. I told my mum I was going to the library to study. She said okay. She didn't look up from the telly. The community centre was a beige building from the 1970s with a leaky roof and a poster for a lost cat that had been there since 2019. Room 17 was down a corridor that smelled of bleach and old tea. The door was open. Inside, about twenty people were sitting on plastic chairs, holding cups of weak orange juice. Mr. Elmwood was at the front. He was wearing a green jumper with a reindeer on it, even though it was March. He smiled when I walked in. "Jasper! Good to see you. Take a seat, take a seat. There's biscuits at the back." I took a seat near the door. I didn't take a biscuit. The session was exactly what I expected. Mr. Elmwood talked about community. He talked about giving back. He talked about the history of the Initiative, how it had started in the 1980s as a small pilot programme and grown into something that supported the whole town. He showed slides with graphs and smiling faces. He talked about the credits: how they paid for the new school roof, how they funded the hospital's children's ward, how they helped elderly people afford their heating bills. "It's a beautiful thing, really," he said. "A community coming together to support its own. We all give a little. We all get a little. That's the deal." A mother raised her hand. She asked if the process was safe. Mr. Elmwood smiled warmly. "Perfectly safe. We follow strict protocols. Every donor is monitored. We never take more than the body can safely replace." Another hand. A father. He asked about the frequency of donations. "It varies," Mr. Elmwood said. "Some donors give monthly. Some give weekly. It depends on the individual's health and the community's needs." I thought about the files in the crate. Donations every three days. The word *yield* in handwriting I couldn't read. I raised my hand. "Yes, Jasper?" "How much blood do you take?" The room went quiet. Mr. Elmwood's smile didn't waver. "That depends on the donor. On average, about four hundred and fifty millilitres per session." "Per session. How many sessions?" "Again, it varies—" "But there's a limit, right? A maximum. A cap." Mr. Elmwood paused. Just for a second. Then the smile came back. "Of course there's a cap. Donor safety is our top priority. We review each case individually. No one is pushed beyond their limits." He said it smoothly. Confidently. But I had seen the files. I had seen the deaths. I had seen the handwriting that said *No error. Protocol followed.* I didn't ask another question. — After the session, people mingled. They talked about the weather. They talked about the school roof. They filled out registration forms. A woman with grey hair and kind eyes approached me. "Are you thinking of signing up, love?" "No." She looked surprised. "Oh. Well, it's a good programme. My granddaughter does it. She's seventeen now. Started when she was fourteen. She's been healthier than ever." "Does she enjoy it?" The woman blinked. "Enjoy it? I don't know if enjoy is the word. But she's proud of it. We all are." I looked at her. She looked sincere. She genuinely believed what she was saying. That was the worst part. I left without taking a form. — I walked home through the dark. The streetlights were off to save power. I remembered my mum telling me about that — the council had turned them off at midnight to save money for the hospital. *It's a small sacrifice,* she'd said. I thought about Maya. I thought about her little brother. I thought about the girl with acne, standing in the corridor, watching people pass. I thought about the crate in the canal, full of records that no one was supposed to find. I got home. My mum was asleep on the sofa. The telly was still on. I turned it off and put a blanket over her. She stirred but didn't wake. I went to my room. I opened my phone. The three red drops were still there. I didn't delete them. But I didn't know what to do with them either. — The next morning, I found Maya before school. "I went to the information session," I said. She didn't look surprised. "I know. They told me." "Who told you?" She didn't answer. She just looked at me with those tired, hollow eyes. "You're in it now," she said. "You can't go back."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] The first and most significant issue with this chapter is that it substantially deviates from the chapter plan. The plan describes Jazz attending the information session, watching Mr. Elmwood present, taking a registration form, and going home — a scene of quiet, unsettling normalcy where no one asks the hard questions and Jazz keeps the form without knowing why. Instead, the chapter introduces entirely new plot elements: a crate of waterlogged files, death records, handwriting that says "No error. Protocol followed," and a canal discovery that is referenced but never shown. Jazz also raises his hand and directly challenges Mr. Elmwood about blood limits, which contradicts the plan's vision of Jazz as a passive observer who watches others nod along. The plan explicitly states "No one asks what happens to the donors long-term. No one asks how much blood is taken." Jazz asks exactly those questions. This is a fundamental failure to follow the chapter plan. The chapter also contradicts the writing plan's broader intentions. The plan states: "No exposition dumps. Maya does not explain the system. Jazz does not research it on Wikipedia. Information comes in fragments, some of it wrong, some of it contradictory." Yet here Jazz has apparently already discovered a crate of death records — a classic thriller plot device that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The plan wanted the horror to be in the system's mundane acceptability, not in discovered evidence of wrongdoing. The chapter undermines this by making the Initiative straightforwardly sinister in a conventional way. The ending — "You're in it now. You can't go back" — is exactly the kind of dramatic closing line the plan warned against. The plan says: "No chapter that ends on a dramatic pause. Some chapters should just stop. Let the reader feel the anticlimax." This ending is a thriller cliché. The dialogue is functional but occasionally weak. Mr. Elmwood's lines are reasonable, but Jazz's confrontational questioning feels out of character for someone described as passive and non-confrontational. The woman with grey hair exists purely to deliver exposition about the programme's acceptability — she's a plot device, not a person. There are some genuine strengths. The community centre atmosphere is rendered with appropriate bleakness (beige building, leaky roof, lost cat poster from 2019, weak orange juice). The detail of the reindeer jumper in March is good character work for Mr. Elmwood. The observation that the woman "genuinely believed what she was saying. That was the worst part" captures the intended tone. The streetlight detail is nicely integrated. However, the chapter's voice is inconsistent with the plan's Jazz. This Jazz is more active, more confrontational, more conventionally heroic. He challenges authority figures directly, he has already discovered damning evidence, he walks home thinking in thriller-protagonist mode. The plan's Jazz would have sat quietly, taken a biscuit, watched people nod, and gone home confused and vaguely complicit. The prose is competent but not distinctive. There are no forced metaphors or purple prose, but there's also little of the dry, deadpan comedy that was supposed to define the voice. The chapter reads more like a competent YA thriller than the darkly comic, understated piece the plan envisioned. The coherence suffers because the crate discovery is referenced but never explained — it appears to belong to a different chapter that wasn't written, making this chapter feel like it's mid-sequence in a different story. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 9 Emotionally Engaging: 10 Compelling Plot: 9 Coherent: 7 Weak Dialogue: 10 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 13 Amateurish: 8 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 8 Characters Consistent with Profile: 7 Followed Chapter Plan: 4 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 11

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: The Launderette I didn't see Maya for a week. She stopped coming to school. Her seat stayed empty. I asked the girl in my form who knew things, and she said Maya had been withdrawn. No explanation. No forwarding address. Just gone. I thought about the crate. I thought about going back to the canal. But I didn't. I don't know why. Maybe I was scared of what I'd find. Maybe I was scared of what I wouldn't. I went to school. I came home. I did my homework. I watched telly with Ella. I lay awake at night staring at the ceiling. The text was still on my phone. I opened it sometimes. Three red drops. I still didn't delete them. — On Saturday, my mum asked me to do the washing. "Please, Jazz. I'm exhausted. The machine's been acting up and I need it done before Monday." I said fine. I took the laundry bag and walked to the launderette on the high street. It was a small place, always damp, always smelling of fabric softener and something vaguely sour. I loaded a machine and sat down to wait. That's when I saw her. Maya. Asleep in the corner behind the dryers. Curled up on a pile of coats with her hood pulled over her face. I stood there for a moment. Then I dropped the laundry basket. She woke up. Flinched. Scrambled backward until she hit the wall. Then she saw me and went still. "You," she said. "You're sleeping in a launderette." "I'm aware." I sat down on the floor across from her. The dryers were loud. Warm. They'd be running for another twenty minutes. "I thought you left," I said. "I did. They found me. Again." She looked worse than before. Her face was thinner. Her eyes had dark circles so deep they looked like bruises. She was wearing the same hoodie she'd been wearing for weeks. It smelled musty even from where I was sitting. "How long have you been here?" "Few days. I ran out of money. I'm waiting for something to open up." "Open up where?" She didn't answer. She pulled her knees up to her chest. She looked small. She looked young. She looked like she hadn't eaten in days. "Have you had food?" She didn't answer. I stood up. "I'm getting you food." "I didn't ask you to—" "I know. I'm doing it anyway." I walked to the corner shop and bought a pasty and a bottle of water. I came back and handed them to her. She stared at them for a long moment. Then she ate the pasty in about thirty seconds, like she was afraid someone would take it away. I sat down again. The dryers hummed. "Why are you here?" she asked. Her voice was quieter now. Less sharp. "I told you. Laundry." "No. Why are you still involved? I told you to stay away. I told you it wasn't safe. I told you everything. And you're still here." I thought about it. I didn't have a good answer. "I don't know," I said. "I think about it all the time. The programme. The files. Your brother. The kids in the black jumpers. I can't stop thinking about it." "So stop." "I can't." She looked at me. Her eyes were wet. She blinked and looked away. "I tried to tell people," she said. "Before I left. I wrote a letter to the local paper. It never got published. I went to the council. They told me the programme was voluntary. They told me I could stop anytime." "Can you?" She laughed. That same short, tired laugh. "You can stop. But they make sure you wish you hadn't." "What does that mean?" She didn't answer. She finished the water. Crushed the bottle. Put it in her pocket. "It means they take things away. Credits. Healthcare. School places. They make it so your family can't afford to live here unless you're part of the system. They make you choose between your family and yourself." She looked at me. "And if you still don't come back, they find you. They bring you back. And then you don't get to leave again." I didn't know what to say. The dryers stopped. The launderette went quiet. "I have to go," she said. She stood up. She was unsteady on her feet. "Where?" "Somewhere else. Somewhere you won't find me." "Maya—" "Don't follow me, Jazz. I mean it. You have a sister. You have a mum. You have a life. Don't throw it away for someone who's already lost theirs." She walked out. The door swung shut behind her. I sat on the floor for a long time, surrounded by warm laundry and the smell of cheap fabric softener. I didn't follow her. I finished the washing. I folded it. I went home. I didn't tell anyone what had happened. I didn't know how to.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates a strong command of the story's established voice and tone. The writing is consistently understated, dry, and emotionally restrained in ways that feel earned rather than evasive. Jazz's passivity is rendered through action rather than declaration — he drops the basket, he buys food anyway, he sits on the floor, he folds the washing and goes home. These are small, specific, physical details that do the work the plan demanded. The chapter follows its plan closely. Maya is found sleeping in the launderette, she's in a deteriorated state, Jazz buys her food, they talk, she explains the system's coercive mechanics (credits, healthcare, school places), and she leaves. The ending note — Jazz sitting on the floor, folding the washing, going home, telling no one — matches the intended anticlimax. The plan's instruction to avoid dramatic chapter endings is honoured; the chapter simply stops. The dialogue is one of the chapter's stronger elements. Maya's lines feel consistent with her profile: brittle, defensive, occasionally sharp, with a dark humour that surfaces briefly ("I'm aware"). The exchange about why Jazz is still involved is handled well — his "I don't know" is exactly the kind of honest non-answer the character would give. The line "Don't throw it away for someone who's already lost theirs" is perhaps the closest the chapter comes to a slightly too-neat formulation, but it's within acceptable range for the character's voice. Where the chapter is slightly weaker is in the exposition delivery. Maya's explanation of how the system coerces people — credits, healthcare, school places — is delivered in a fairly direct, expository block. The plan explicitly warned against exposition dumps, and while this isn't a full dump, it's the most tell-heavy passage in the chapter. The information is necessary, but the delivery is a little flat in a way that reads as functional rather than artful. The plan's instruction that "information comes in fragments, some of it wrong, some of it contradictory" is only partially honoured here — Maya's explanation is fairly coherent and complete, which slightly undercuts the intended fragmentation. The physical details are well-chosen: the smell of fabric softener and something vaguely sour, the pasty eaten in thirty seconds, the crushed water bottle put in her pocket. These are grounded and specific without being overwrought. The dryers stopping to create a moment of quiet is a small structural choice that works without being heavy-handed. The chapter's emotional register is appropriate. There's genuine pathos in Maya's deterioration — the thin face, the dark circles, the hunger — without the writing becoming sentimental. Jazz's helplessness is rendered through his small, inadequate gestures (buying a pasty, sitting on the floor) rather than through internal anguish. This is consistent with his character profile. One minor inconsistency: the plan mentions Jazz thinking about "the crate" and "going back to the canal," but "the crate" hasn't appeared in the preceding chapter summaries. This appears to be a continuity error — possibly a detail from a draft that didn't make it into the final plan. It's a small thing but slightly jarring. The chapter's ending note — "I didn't tell anyone what had happened. I didn't know how to." — is appropriately flat and exhausted, consistent with the story's tonal mandate. It doesn't reach for profundity. It just stops. Overall, this is a competent, well-executed chapter that honours the story's voice and plan. It doesn't transcend its material, but it doesn't betray it either. The main weakness is the slightly expository quality of Maya's explanation, and a minor continuity error. The strengths are the physical specificity, the consistent voice, and the earned emotional restraint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: The Three of Them I didn't go looking for Maya. I told myself it was because she'd asked me not to. I told myself it was because I had my own life to worry about. I told myself a lot of things. None of them felt true. A week passed. Then two. I stopped checking my phone for messages from unknown numbers. I stopped thinking about the crate in the canal. I stopped looking over my shoulder for kids in black jumpers. Almost. — It was a Thursday, late. My mum was working nights. Ella was asleep. I was on the sofa watching something I wasn't really watching, when I heard a knock at the door. I didn't move at first. It was nearly eleven. No one knocked at our door at eleven. The knock came again. Harder. I got up. I looked through the peephole. Three of them. Standing on my doorstep in their black jumpers. The girl with acne. The boy with the cold. A third one I didn't recognise — a tall kid with a shaved head and a blank face. I opened the door. Not all the way. Just enough to talk. "What do you want?" The girl stepped forward. She looked different than the last time I'd seen her. Tenser. Older. "We need to talk." "It's eleven o'clock." "I know what time it is." She looked past me, into the house. I stepped out onto the doorstep and pulled the door closed behind me. "Talk fast." She glanced at the other two. The boy with the cold nodded. She took a breath. "You've been looking into the programme. You went to the information session. You've been meeting with Maya. We know about the crate." My stomach dropped. "We're not here to stop you," she said. "We're here to warn you." "Warn me about what?" The boy with the cold spoke. His voice was rough, like he'd been sick recently. "There's a limit. If a donor goes over the limit, the system flags them. And then they have to be dealt with." "Dealt with how?" No one answered. "Maya went over the limit," the girl said. "That's why she's running. That's why they keep finding her." "What is the limit?" The girl looked at the ground. The boy looked at the sky. The tall one stared at nothing. "I don't know the exact number," she said. "But I know it's lower than you'd think." I felt cold. I thought about the files. The donations every three days. The deaths. "Why are you telling me this?" The girl met my eyes. For a second, she looked like she might cry. Then she blinked and it was gone. "Because I have a little sister too." She turned. The other two followed. They walked down the path and out into the dark street. I watched them go. They didn't look back. I went inside. I locked the door. I stood in the hallway for a long time, not moving. — The next morning, I went to the canal. The crate was still there. I pulled it up. I opened the lid. I took out a handful of files and sat on the damp grass and read. I read about a girl named Sarah. Fourteen. Donated weekly for eighteen months. Then every five days. Then every three. The last entry said *Yield: decreased. Recommend increased frequency.* The next page said *DECEASED — SYSTEM ERROR.* I read about a boy named Daniel. Fifteen. Same pattern. Same cause of death. Same handwriting: *No error. Protocol followed.* I read about Maya. Her name was in the files. She was listed as *Status: Absconded.* Underneath, in red ink: *Subject is to be retrieved and returned to programme. Priority: High.* I read about her brother. He was listed too. *Age: 8. Status: Active.* His donation schedule was every two weeks. His yield was marked *Below average.* There was a note in the margin: *Monitor closely. Consider increased frequency.* I closed the file.
