He smells like cedar and something artificial, cologne he picked up at an airport. **Allegiances:** He's loyal to his mother, who still lives in Cardiff and refuses to take his money. He's loyal to his agent, a woman named Priya who's been with him since drama school and who he trusts more than anyone. He's loyal to the idea of Wales, or at least to the Welshness he performs when it's useful. He has no close friends—didn't keep any from before fame, and the ones he's made since are industry people who want things from him. His last relationship was with a co-star, and it ended so badly he's still cleaning up the mess. **Quotes:** - (To Elin, early) "I'm not very good at being a person people want to talk to." *Pause.* "That's the truest thing I've said in six months." - (Internal, after she doesn't react to his name) *She doesn't care. She really doesn't care. Why does that make me want to tell her everything?* - (To Dafydd the photographer, cornered) "You went to school with her brother. She used to read in the library at lunch because she didn't have friends. That's not a story you get to sell." - (Internal, alone in the B&B) *I could leave. I should leave. There's a flight to Prague tomorrow. I could be someone else by Thursday.* --- # Character 2: Elin Prothero **Background:** Born in Llanfawr, 1990. Only child of a fisherman (died at sea when she was twelve) and a receptionist at the local GP surgery (still alive, still in Llanfawr, still asking when Elin will "settle down"). She left at eighteen for Cardiff University, studied English Literature, stayed for a decade. Worked at Waterstones, then at a small press doing editorial, then at nothing for a while because she was depressed and didn't know how to name it. Her aunt Gwyneth owned Y Stori Fach. When Gwyneth died in 2021, Elin inherited the shop and moved back. She thought it would be temporary. It's been three years. Her relationship with Angharad ended six months before she moved back—a slow dissolution, not a rupture. They still text on birthdays. **Personality:** Elin is quiet in a way that reads as cold, but she's not cold. She's watchful. She takes her time before speaking because she wants to say something worth hearing, and she's learned that most conversation is noise. She has a dry wit that emerges when she's comfortable; strangers think she's serious, friends know she's funny. She's deeply uncomfortable with attention—being looked at, photographed, discussed. She built a small life in Llanfawr precisely because no one looks at her here. She reads constantly, promiscuously, and has strong opinions about books that she'll share if asked but never volunteers. She's kind in small, practical ways—she remembers that Mrs. Davies likes her tea strong, she orders books for customers she knows will never buy them. She's never learned how to accept help. **Idiosyncrasies:** - She arranges things obsessively. The books on her counter are always at right angles. The mugs are hung by colour. She doesn't see it as neatness; she sees it as necessary. - She talks to the shop cat (a grey stray named Bronwen who adopted her) in full sentences, as if expecting answers. - She bites the inside of her cheek when she's holding something back. She does this constantly in Chapter 1. - She has a habit of disappearing—she'll leave a conversation without saying goodbye because she's tired and can't summon the energy for the social chore. - She's never learned to take a compliment. She deflects, changes the subject, or makes a self-deprecating joke that lands wrong. **Flaws:** - She's avoidant. When things get hard, she goes quiet and waits for them to pass. It worked for the grief after her father; it worked for the end with Angharad. It's not a strategy, it's a reflex. - She judges people quickly and fully. Her first impression is usually final. This has cost her friendships and opportunities. She knows this and can't stop. - She's proud in a way that looks like independence but is really fear of rejection. She won't ask for what she wants because she's already braced for the no. - She romanticizes loneliness. She's built an identity around being alone, around not needing people, and she doesn't know who she'd be if she let someone in. - She has a short fuse for people who perform, which is ironic because she's performing stoicism constantly. She just doesn't see it as performance. **Physicality:** Average height (five-five), solid build, the kind of body that's never been the center of anyone's attention and has learned to take up only the space it needs. She has dark hair she cuts herself, once a season, in the bathroom mirror. Her hands are always busy—straightening, dusting, holding a book. She makes eye contact when she speaks but looks away when she listens; it's the opposite of most people and throws them off. She dresses in layers: jumpers, cardigans, boots that have lasted through three winters. She doesn't wear makeup. She has a small scar above her left eyebrow from a childhood fall. She's never thought about it as an adult. She thinks her face is forgettable, which has always been a comfort. **Allegiances:** Her mother, with difficulty (they love each other but don't know how to talk). The memory of her father, who she still misses in sharp, unpredictable pangs. Her aunt Gwyneth, whose handwriting is still on the price stickers in the shop and who Elin talks to when she's alone at closing time. Angharad, distantly, with residual fondness and no desire to reconnect. The shop itself—she thinks of Y Stori Fach as a living thing she's responsible for. She has three friends from Cardiff she texts in a group chat that's mostly silent now. She tells herself she doesn't mind. **Quotes:** - (To Rhys, early) "You're trying too hard." *He laughs, surprised.* "I mean it as a kindness. You don't have to work so hard." - (Internal, after he leaves the first time) *That was strange. He's strange. I hope he doesn't come back.* (She hopes he comes back.) - (To herself, alone in the shop, unprompted) "You don't get to be the person someone runs to. You're the person they leave behind." *She shakes her head, goes back to work.* - (To Rhys, later) "I'm not a chapter in your story. I'm not a lesson you learn. I'm just a person who makes you tea and reads too much. If that's not enough, you know where the door is." --- # Character 3: Dafydd Thomas (Supporting) **Background:** Born in Llanfawr, 1978. Went to school with Elin's older brother (who now lives in Australia and sends Christmas cards). Left for Cardiff at eighteen, trained as a photographer, spent twenty years shooting weddings and corporate events. Three years ago, he sold a photo of a minor royal at a Cardiff petrol station to a tabloid and discovered there was more money in chasing celebrities than documenting brides. He's not proud of it. He has a daughter, twelve, who lives with her mother in Swansea. He sees her every other weekend. He drinks too much but not enough to call it a problem. **Function in the story:** Dafydd is not a villain. He's a local man doing a job he's ambivalent about, and he knows Elin's family history. When he takes the photo, he feels bad about it. When Elin confronts him (off-page, between chapters 6 and 7), he offers to not sell it if she asks him not to. She doesn't ask. This complicates him—he's not predatory, he's desperate and tired. **Quote:** (To Elin, in the shop) "I know it's him. I just need a photo, Elin. It's my job. I've got a daughter who needs new school shoes. You understand." *He doesn't say it to manipulate her. He says it because it's true.*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: The Door The bell above the door was too loud for such a small shop. It had been too loud for thirty years, and Elin had never thought to replace it. The jangling cut through the quiet like a warning. She looked up from the biography she was pricing. A man stood in the doorway, rain streaming from his coat, his chest heaving. He was beautiful in that obvious way—cheekbones you could hang a career on, dark hair plastered to his forehead, eyes that found her across the room and held on. He looked like someone who was used to being looked at. “Is there a back exit?” he said. Elin blinked. “No.” “A toilet. A cupboard. A—anything. A trapdoor.” “It’s a bookshop.” “I know it’s a—look.” He stepped forward, and she saw the panic beneath the composure. His hands were shaking slightly. “There’s a photographer. He’s been following me since Cardiff. I just need five minutes.” She studied him. The rain was still dripping off his coat onto her floor. He was wearing an expensive jacket, the kind that cost more than her rent, and boots that had never seen a Welsh winter before today. He was clearly someone. She didn’t care to place him. “You can wait,” she said, and returned to her pricing. The silence stretched. She could feel him standing there, unsure what to do with himself. She heard him shift his weight, heard the rain drip from his sleeve onto the floorboards. She did not look up. “That’s it?” he said finally. “What’s it?” “You’re not going to ask who I am?” “You’ll tell me if you want me to know.” He made a sound—half laugh, half exhale. She glanced up. He was staring at her with something that looked like confusion. She had seen that look before, on people who expected a different reaction and didn’t know what to do with the one they got. “Rhys,” he said. “Rhys Caradog.” He said it like it meant something. She supposed it did, somewhere else. She said, “Elin,” and returned to her pricing. “Elin,” he repeated. “Elin. That’s a good name.” “It’s Welsh. You should recognise it.” He laughed again, surprised. “I’m Welsh.” “So you say.” He came closer to the counter. His coat was still dripping, and she noticed he was careful not to lean against anything. He wasn’t trying to make himself at home. He was hovering, uncertain, like a man who’d walked into the wrong room and couldn’t figure out how to back out gracefully. “It’s a beautiful shop,” he said. “It’s a shop.” “How long have you had it?” “Three years.” “Before that?” “I worked in Cardiff.” “What did you do?” She looked up at him properly then. He was trying too hard. His questions had the rhythm of someone who was used to charming information out of people, the way other people asked for the time. She wondered if he even noticed he was doing it. “I worked in publishing,” she said. “You don’t have to make conversation with me.” He blinked. “What?” “You’re hiding from a photographer. You don’t have to pretend you’re here for the books. You can just stand there and breathe until it’s safe to leave. I won’t be offended.” He stared at her. She watched him try several responses in his head and discard them. Finally he said, “That was really direct.” “I’m Welsh. So I’m told.” His mouth twitched. For a moment, the performance dropped. The charming mask slipped, and underneath it he looked almost young—tired, exposed, grateful for someone who wasn’t playing along. “I don’t know how to do that,” he said quietly. “Just stand. Without talking.” “Try.” He did. He stood by the counter, hands in his pockets, and was silent. The rain drummed on the roof. A car passed. The old radiator clicked. She pretended to read the spine of the biography, but she was watching him peripherally, and she saw his hands relax, saw his shoulders drop a fraction of an inch. Two minutes passed. Three. “I don’t even know what I’m running from anymore,” he said. “The photographer, or what people are saying, or just—myself. I don’t know.” She did not respond. She was not going to be drawn in. He looked at her, and she felt the weight of his attention. “Do you know who I am?” She thought about lying. She thought about saying no, because it would be easier, because it would keep him at the distance she preferred. But somewhere in her chest, something pulled. “I know you’re famous,” she said. “I know there was something in the papers recently. I don’t read tabloids, so I don’t know what it was, and I don’t care.” “You don’t care?” His voice cracked on the second word. “Should I?” He looked at her for a long moment. Then he did something unexpected. He smiled—a real smile, not the charming one, small and crooked and surprised. “No,” he said. “You shouldn’t.” He looked at his watch, then toward the window. The rain had eased. “I think he’s gone.” She didn’t say anything. He looked at the counter, at the stack of books she’d been pricing. “I should buy something. For the trouble.” “You don’t have to.” “I want to.” He scanned the shelves quickly, almost desperately, and she watched him pick up a book and put it down, pick up another, put it down. He was going to grab something at random, she could see, something that looked intellectual enough to match the performance. “Top shelf,” she said. “Left side. Blue spine.” He found it. A slim volume, worn. *Selected Poems.* R.S. Thomas. “That’s Welsh,” he said. “So I’m told.” He looked at the cover, then back at her. “Will you tell me why you picked this one?” “No.” He laughed again. He pulled out his wallet and placed a twenty-pound note on the counter. She took it, gave him change, and slid the book across to him in a paper bag. “Elin,” he said, testing it again. His own card was on the counter now, between them. She wasn’t sure when he’d put it there. “I’ll be back. To thank you properly.” “You don’t have to.” “I know I don’t have to.” He picked up the book. “Thank you for not pretending.” He left. The bell jangled. The door swung shut. Elin stood alone in the quiet. She looked at his card on the counter. Rhys Caradog, with a London number and an email she didn’t need. She slid it into the drawer under the till. She didn’t know why she didn’t throw it away.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. The exchange between Elin and Rhys has genuine texture and rhythm. Elin's clipped responses ("It's a bookshop," "It's a shop," "Three years") feel earned rather than affected — they establish character through economy rather than explanation. Rhys's escalating attempts to provoke a reaction ("That's it?" / "You're not going to ask who I am?") feel true to the profile of a man whose ego requires constant feeding. The line "You'll tell me if you want me to know" is particularly good — it's the kind of thing a genuinely self-possessed person says, not a performed version of self-possession. The "So you say" / "So I'm told" callback is a nice piece of craft — it establishes a private register between them without announcing itself as significant. **Character Consistency** Rhys's name-repetition tic ("Elin. Elin. That's a good name.") is deployed exactly as planned. His hovering uncertainty, his attempt to charm through questions, his genuine surprise when the mask slips — all consistent with the profile. The moment where "the charming mask slipped, and underneath it he looked almost young" is shown through behavior rather than stated, which is the right instinct. Elin's watchfulness, her dry deflections, her internal resistance to being drawn in — all consistent. The detail of her "pretending to read the spine of the biography" while watching him peripherally is good physical specificity. Her internal conflict is conveyed through behavior (sliding the card into the drawer, the final line) without being named. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely. The key beats are all present: the panic entrance, the back-exit request, the name introduction, the R.S. Thomas recommendation, the card left on the counter, the drawer. The plan called for him to say the scandal was overblown and that "the woman was lying" — this is notably absent from the chapter, which is actually an improvement. The plan's version would have made Rhys less sympathetic and more complicated in ways that might have cluttered the first chapter. The chapter wisely keeps the scandal vague. The plan specified Rhys would repeat her name "twice" — the chapter gives us "Elin. Elin. That's a good name," which is three beats and works better. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. "Cheekbones you could hang a career on" is a good line — specific, slightly sardonic, in Elin's register. The physical details are well-chosen: the dripping coat, the expensive jacket that "had never seen a Welsh winter before today," the radiator clicking. These are specific without being labored. The opening paragraph about the bell is slightly over-explained ("It had been too loud for thirty years, and Elin had never thought to replace it") — the second sentence is unnecessary and slightly weakens the impact of the first. The bell's significance is better left as pure atmosphere. **Weaknesses** The line "He was beautiful in that obvious way — cheekbones you could hang a career on, dark eyes that found her across the room and held on" is the chapter's most self-conscious moment. "Eyes that found her across the room and held on" is a mild cliché of romantic fiction. It's not egregious, but it's the one place where the prose reaches for effect rather than precision. "He looked like someone who was used to being looked at" — this is a good observation but slightly on-the-nose given that we're about to spend the chapter establishing exactly this. The moment where Rhys says "I don't even know what I'm running from anymore... myself. I don't know" comes slightly early and slightly easily. The plan called for this kind of revelation to be earned over multiple chapters. Here it arrives in the first scene, during the silence, and while it's not implausible, it feels like a small acceleration. The plan's version of Rhys is more guarded; this Rhys opens up a fraction too readily. The final line — "She didn't know why she didn't throw it away" — is the plan's line almost verbatim, and it works, but it's the one moment where the chapter feels like it's executing a blueprint rather than discovering something. It's a good ending, but it's a slightly announced good ending. **Overall Assessment** This is accomplished work. The dialogue is the best thing here — genuinely differentiated voices, genuine tension, genuine wit. The physical specificity is mostly excellent. The characters are consistent with their profiles. The chapter follows its plan faithfully while making one or two smart departures. The prose is controlled without being cold. The romantic tension is present without being underlined. The weaknesses are minor: one or two clichéd phrases, a slightly premature emotional revelation, an ending that's good but slightly telegraphed. This is the work of a writer who knows what they're doing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: The Return Three days passed. She didn't think about him. She thought about the leak in the stockroom ceiling that needed fixing, and the order of poetry books that hadn't arrived, and the way Bronwen had scratched the armchair again. She did not think about the card in the drawer. On the fourth morning, the bell jangled, and there he was. He was drier this time. His coat was open, revealing a navy jumper that looked expensive and soft. His hair was combed. He was trying to look casual, and failing. "I came back for a proper book," he said. "The R.S. Thomas wasn't proper?" "It was proper. I just—" He stopped. "I haven't read it yet." "I know." He blinked. "How do you know?" "Because you'd have wanted to talk about it if you had. That's what people do when they read something that moves them. They find someone to tell." He considered this. "What if it didn't move me?" "Then you'd have put it down and not come back." He leaned against the counter, closer than he should have. He smelled like rain and soap, not the cologne from before. "You're very sure about people." "I'm very sure about readers." "And I'm not a reader?" "You didn't read the book." He opened his mouth, closed it. She watched him struggle for a response and felt a small, private satisfaction. She returned to the invoices she'd been sorting. He didn't leave. She could feel him standing there, waiting. The silence stretched longer than was comfortable, and she knew he was testing her, waiting for her to fill it. She didn't. "I'm not good at sitting still," he said finally. "I never have been. My mother used to say I was born running." "Mothers say things like that." "Did yours?" She looked up. His question had landed somewhere unexpected. "No," she said. "My mother didn't say things like that." He waited. She didn't elaborate. "I'm sorry," he said. "I didn't mean to pry." "You didn't pry. I just don't feel the need to fill every silence with words." He winced. "That was aimed at me." "Was it?" "I don't know. Was it?" She almost smiled. She caught herself. "You wanted a proper book. What are you looking for?" "I don't know." He ran a hand through his hair. "Something that will make me feel less like I'm rattling around inside my own head." "That's specific." "Is that a problem?" "No." She moved from behind the counter and walked to the fiction section. He followed. She could feel him behind her, too close, his presence taking up more space than his body should. She pulled a novel from the shelf. *The Loneliness of the Long-Distance Runner.* "Have you read this?" "No." "It's about a boy who runs away from everything because he doesn't know how to stay. He thinks running is freedom. It's not." He took the book from her hands. His fingers brushed hers. She didn't pull away, but she didn't react either. He looked at the cover, then at her. "You're calling me out." "I'm recommending a book." "Same thing." She returned to the counter without answering. He followed. She noticed the way he moved—not gracefully, exactly, but with an actor's awareness of his own body, as if he was always on a stage. "I'll read this one," he said. "I promise." "You said that about the last one." "I know." He met her eyes. "I mean it this time." She didn't say anything. He placed the book on the counter. She rang it up. He paid with exact change, which surprised her. She'd expected another twenty, another performance of generosity. At the door, he paused. "Can I ask you something?" "You just did." He smiled. She saw the charm rising again, the practised warmth, and then she saw him catch himself and push it down. Interesting. "Why did you close the shop early that day? The first day." "I told you. I don't read tabloids." "That's not an answer." "It's the only one you're getting." He looked at her for a long moment. She held his gaze. She wasn't going to look away first. "Elin," he said. "I'll be back." "You said that." "I know." He opened the door. The bell jangled. "I meant it." He left. She stood behind the counter, her hands gripping the edge. She could feel her heart beating in her throat, and she didn't know why. She hadn't said anything she regretted. She hadn't given him anything. Except the book. Except the recommendation. Except the conversation she told herself she didn't want. She heard her mother's voice in her head, from years ago: *You build walls and then you're surprised when no one climbs over them.* She shook her head, cleared the thought, and went back to her invoices. Bronwen jumped onto the counter and meowed. "I know," Elin said. "I don't know what I'm doing either." The cat blinked at her, then lay down on the invoices. Elin let her. That evening, after closing, she took the drawer out from under the till. The card was still there. Rhys Caradog. London number. She looked at it for a long time. She put it back in the drawer. She did not write down the number. She did not throw it away.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this story demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on the ambitious intentions laid out in the writing plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The sparring between Rhys and Elin feels genuinely earned rather than constructed. Exchanges like "You're calling me out." / "I'm recommending a book." / "Same thing." have the rhythm of real conversation where subtext does the heavy lifting. The dialogue avoids the trap of being too witty or too on-the-nose simultaneously — it lands in a productive middle space. Rhys's verbal patterns (filling silence, asking questions, performing and then catching himself) are consistent with his profile. Elin's short answers and refusal to elaborate feel authentic to her character. The moment where Rhys "catches himself" pushing down the charm is particularly well-handled — it's shown through behavior rather than narration, and it's the kind of specific observation that rewards attentive reading. **Character Consistency** Both characters are well-rendered against their profiles. Rhys's name-repetition tic ("Elin") appears naturally. His inability to sit in silence, his over-explanation, his mother anecdote that slides toward performance — all consistent. Elin's watchfulness, her dry deflections, her internal state revealed only through small physical details (gripping the counter edge, catching herself before smiling) — these are faithful to the profile. The cat dialogue at the end ("I don't know what I'm doing either") is a nice touch that matches her established habit of talking to Bronwen in full sentences. **Following the Chapter Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely. The key beats are hit: Rhys returns, claims he wants a proper book, hasn't read the Thomas, tries charm, drops it, says something true ("I'm not very good at this" is slightly reframed but present in spirit), she recommends a second book about someone who can't stay, he promises to read it. The Mrs. Davies interruption from the plan is absent — this is a notable deviation. The plan specified a customer interruption that gives Elin visible relief, which would have added texture and shown her social discomfort more concretely. Its absence is a minor loss. The ending with the card in the drawer is preserved and works well. **Show vs. Tell** The chapter is predominantly showing rather than telling, which is commendable. "She almost smiled. She caught herself." is efficient and revealing. The physical detail of exact change surprising her (versus the expected performance of generosity) is excellent — it shows character through behavior without commentary. However, there are a few moments where the narration edges toward telling: "She could feel him behind her, too close, his presence taking up more space than his body should" is slightly over-explained. The reader can infer this from context. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. It avoids purple prose almost entirely. The mother's voice interjection ("You build walls and then you're surprised when no one climbs over them") is the one moment that feels slightly too neat — a little too thematically convenient, a little too on-the-nose as a piece of character exposition. It's the chapter's weakest moment, edging toward the kind of emotional politeness the plan explicitly warned against. The book recommendation — *The Loneliness of the Long-Distance Runner* — is a good choice, but Elin's explanation of it ("It's about a boy who runs away from everything because he doesn't know how to stay... He thinks running is freedom. It's not.") is slightly too explicit. The plan called for "a novel about a man who comes home and doesn't fit anymore" — the actual choice is thematically adjacent but the explanation over-telegraphs the parallel to Rhys. A more oblique recommendation would have been truer to Elin's character as someone who doesn't volunteer her opinions. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally engaging in a quiet, restrained way that suits the material. The tension is maintained throughout. The ending — the card in the drawer, the deliberate non-action — lands well. The reader is invested in both characters and in the question of what happens next. **Creativity and Surprise** The chapter is competent and well-crafted but not particularly surprising. The beats follow a predictable will-they-won't-they rhythm. The book recommendation as a vehicle for subtext is a familiar device. The chapter does what it sets out to do without transcending it. The "exact change" detail is the most genuinely surprising and specific moment. **Overall Assessment** This is skilled, controlled writing that serves the story well. It's not a masterpiece — it has moments of over-explanation and one or two too-convenient thematic gestures — but it's consistently good and occasionally excellent. The dialogue is the standout achievement. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Walk A week passed. She told herself she wasn't counting the days. She told herself she wasn't waiting for the bell to jangle. She told herself a lot of things. On Thursday afternoon, he walked in. He was holding the book she'd recommended, dog-eared and clearly read. He didn't say anything. He just placed it on the counter. She looked at it. Then at him. "Well?" she said. He took a breath. "The boy who runs. He thinks he's escaping, but he's not. He's just moving the pain somewhere else. He doesn't know how to stop because stopping means feeling it." She waited. "The scene where he's in the field, lying on his back, looking at the sky. He realises he's been running from himself, not from the borstal, not from the law. And he doesn't know what to do with that knowledge. So he keeps running anyway." He stopped. He looked at her like he was waiting for a verdict. She picked up the book and flipped through it. The spine was creased. He'd read it properly. She hadn't expected that. "Why did you recommend it?" he asked. "Really." "Because I thought you'd see yourself in it." "And I did." He paused. "Is that what you think of me? Someone who runs?" "I think you're someone who doesn't know what he's running from anymore. That's different." He let out a breath. "Yeah. I suppose it is." Mrs. Davies walked in. The moment broke. Elin helped her find the new crime novel, answered three questions about the author's previous books, and watched her leave. When she turned back, Rhys was still there. "I read the poems too," he said quietly. "The R.S. Thomas?" "Every one of them. The one about the bright field—I read it three times. I don't know if I understood it, but I felt something." She didn't know what to say. No one had ever come back to talk to her about the books she recommended. She recommended them, people bought them, and she never heard about them again. That was the arrangement. "I'm closing early," she said. "Excuse me?" "I'm closing early. Give me five minutes." She did the till, locked the stockroom, turned the sign to closed. He watched her the whole time, not offering to help, as if he was afraid any movement would shatter whatever was happening. She grabbed her coat. "Come on." They walked to the seafront. The wind was brutal, coming off the Irish Sea with nothing to stop it. He pulled his collar up. She shoved her hands in her pockets. They walked in silence for a while. "I asked you before," he said. "Why you came back to Llanfawr." "I remember." "You didn't answer." "I answered enough." They reached the pier. The waves were grey and heavy, slapping against the stones. She stopped at the railing and looked out at the horizon. He stood beside her, close enough that she could feel the warmth of him through the cold. "I was with someone in Cardiff," she said. "Her name was Angharad. It ended badly." "How badly?" "Badly enough that I needed to leave. Badly enough that I came back to a town I swore I'd never live in again." She paused. "Badly enough that I still dream about her, and I wake up angry." He didn't say anything. He didn't offer comfort. She appreciated that. "So I came back," she said. "And I took over my aunt's shop. And I've been hiding here for three years, pretending it's a choice." "Is it?" "No. But I tell myself it is. That's what hiding looks like." He laughed—a short, surprised sound. "You're the most honest person I've ever met." "That's a low bar. You're an actor." "I don't think that's why." She turned to look at him. His face was open in a way she hadn't seen before. The charm was gone. The performance was gone. He was just a man standing in the cold, looking at her like she was something he didn't understand and wanted to. "But you're hiding too," she said. "Was it the running book that gave it away?" "The way you came into my shop. The way you talk. The way you fill silence because you're afraid of what might surface if you don't." He looked down at his hands. "I've been playing a part for so long I don't know what's underneath it anymore. I don't know if there's anything underneath it." "There is." "How do you know?" "Because you read the poems. And you came back to tell me about them." He looked at her. The wind whipped her hair across her face. He reached out, hesitated, then tucked a strand behind her ear. His fingers lingered on her cheek. She didn't move away. He leaned in. She felt the pull of it, the gravity. She could feel his breath on her skin. She could see the want in his eyes, raw and unguarded. A gull screamed overhead. The moment broke. He pulled back. His hand fell to his side. "I want to kiss you," he said. "But I think if I do, I'll ruin this." "Then don't." He nodded. He didn't argue. He didn't try to charm his way back into the moment. He just stood there, letting the silence settle. They walked back to the shop without speaking. The rain started when they were half a block away. By the time they reached the door, they were both soaked. She unlocked the door. The bell jangled. He stood on the threshold. "Elin." "Yes." "Thank you. For the walk. For the books. For—" He gestured vaguely. "This." "Thank you for reading them." He smiled. It was small, tired, and real. "I'll see you tomorrow." It wasn't a question. She didn't tell him not to come. She closed the door and leaned against it, her heart hammering. Bronwen appeared and rubbed against her ankles. She slid down to the floor and sat there, wet and cold and completely undone. She had not felt anything like this in years. She did not know what to do with it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this piece represents a confident, largely successful execution of the plan and prompt. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is generally strong and distinct. Rhys's opening analysis of the running book is specific and earned—he's clearly read it, and his interpretation ("He thinks he's escaping, but he's not. He's just moving the pain somewhere else") functions both as literary commentary and self-revelation without being heavy-handed. Elin's responses are appropriately terse and watchful. The exchange "Why did you recommend it? Really." / "Because I thought you'd see yourself in it." is clean and effective. However, there are moments where the dialogue becomes slightly too neat. "You're the most honest person I've ever met." / "That's a low bar. You're an actor." is a good line, but it's the kind of line that feels slightly written—a bit too polished for the naturalistic register the piece is otherwise aiming for. Similarly, "I've been playing a part for so long I don't know what's underneath it anymore" is a touch on-the-nose, a line Rhys might say in a film about himself rather than in an unguarded moment. The plan specifically warned against emotional politeness and characters saying things that land wrong; this line lands too smoothly. **Character Consistency** Rhys is largely consistent with his profile. His name-repetition tic is absent here (understandably, since they're past introductions), and his jaw-touching nervous gesture doesn't appear, which is a minor missed opportunity for physical specificity. His genuine engagement with the books is well-rendered. The moment where he "didn't offer to help, as if he was afraid any movement would shatter whatever was happening" is a nice behavioral observation. Elin is well-rendered. Her disclosure about Angharad is appropriately spare and specific. The detail that she "still dreams about her, and wakes up angry" is excellent—it's precise and emotionally true without being overwrought. Her sliding down the door at the end with Bronwen is consistent with her character and avoids sentimentality. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely. The book discussion happens, the walk to the seafront happens, Elin's disclosure about Angharad and Cardiff happens, Rhys's confession about not knowing what's real happens, the almost-kiss happens, and the restrained ending happens. The plan specified "He wants to kiss her. She can see him wanting it. He doesn't." The chapter executes this but makes it slightly more explicit—he says "I want to kiss you"—which is less subtle than the plan's version but still functional. The plan's version (wanting visible but unspoken) would have been stronger. **Show vs. Tell** There are some tell-don't-show moments. "The charm was gone. The performance was gone. He was just a man standing in the cold, looking at her like she was something he didn't understand and wanted to." This is telling us what we should be seeing. The chapter would be stronger if this were conveyed through behavior rather than narration. Similarly, "She had not felt anything like this in years" at the end is a direct statement of emotional state that the preceding scene had already earned without needing to be named. **Physical Specificity** The plan called for physical specificity—"the weight of a book in the hand, the sound of the bell, the smell of rain on wool." The chapter delivers some of this: the dog-eared spine, the wind off the Irish Sea, the rain starting half a block from the shop. The gull screaming overhead to break the almost-kiss moment is effective and specific. However, the chapter could use more of the granular physical detail the plan promised. The pier scene is somewhat generic in its staging. **Pacing and Structure** The pacing is good. The interruption by Mrs. Davies is well-placed and functions as the plan intended. The transition from shop to seafront is handled efficiently. The ending—Elin on the floor, wet and cold, Bronwen at her ankles—is earned and appropriately understated. **Unsurprising Elements** The almost-kiss on the pier is a familiar romance beat, and while the execution is competent, it doesn't subvert expectations in the way the plan's more ambitious notes suggested. The "gull screamed overhead" interruption is a slightly convenient device. The chapter is good but not surprising—it delivers what it promises without exceeding it. **Purple Prose / Forced Metaphor** The prose is largely clean. "The waves were grey and heavy, slapping against the stones" is functional. There's no significant purple prose or forced metaphor, which is a genuine achievement given the romantic register. **Overall Assessment** This is a well-crafted chapter that executes its plan faithfully and maintains consistent, believable characters. Its weaknesses are: occasional over-smooth dialogue, some telling where showing would serve better, and a slight conventionality in the almost-kiss scene. It doesn't quite reach the level of the plan's most ambitious notes—the "ragged" growth, the "uncomfortable silences and missed cues"—but it's a solid, emotionally engaging piece of romantic fiction with a distinct voice. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: The Photographer Another week passed. He came to the shop every day. Some days he bought a book. Some days he just sat in the armchair by the window and read, and she worked around him as if he were part of the furniture. He didn't try to charm her. He didn't fill the silence. He just stayed. On Tuesday, Dafydd Thomas walked in. Elin recognised him immediately. He was older than she remembered, his hair gone grey, his face lined in ways that suggested a hard life rather than a long one. He used to come to her mother's house for dinner, back when her brother still lived here. He'd been nice then. He'd been a boy with a camera, always taking photos of everything. He wasn't taking photos now. He was holding a book he'd picked up near the door, pretending to read the back cover. "Dafydd," she said. He looked up. He had the decency to look uncomfortable. "Elin. You look well." "I'm not going to help you." "I haven't asked you to." "You're going to." He sighed and put the book down. "I know he's been here. I saw him going into the chippy two nights ago. I just need a photo, Elin. It's my job." "Your job is taking pictures of people who don't want to be photographed?" "Right now, my job is paying my daughter's school fees." He said it flatly, without self-pity. "I know you don't owe me anything. But I'm asking you to look the other way." She thought about the man in the armchair, who had read her aunt's old copy of *The Collected Poems* and come back to talk about it. She thought about the way he'd tucked her hair behind her ear on the pier. "No," she said. Dafydd nodded slowly. "I figured you'd say that." He walked to the door, paused. "I'll get the shot eventually. He's not careful. He thinks Llanfawr is safe. It's not." He left. That evening, Rhys arrived at the shop after closing. He knocked on the glass, and she let him in. His face was pale. He didn't take off his coat. "Dafydd Thomas," he said. "The photographer. He was here." "Earlier today." "I know. My driver saw him outside the B&B." He ran his hands through his hair. "I need to leave. I should leave tonight." "Where will you go?" "Prague. I've got a film starting next month. I could move the dates forward, stay in a hotel, wait for this to blow over." "Then go." He looked at her. "Is that what you want?" "I want you to do what you need to do." "That's not an answer." "It's the only one I have." He stood in the middle of her shop, looking lost. She watched him cycle through options, discard them, start again. This was a man who was used to solving problems by moving, by leaving, by becoming someone else in a different city. But he wasn't moving now. "Can I stay?" he asked. "Tonight?" "Just tonight. I'll leave in the morning. I just need—" He stopped. "I need one night where no one is taking my picture. One night where I'm not running." She should have said no. She knew she should have said no. This was how things got complicated. This was how walls came down. "Upstairs," she said. "The sofa folds out. I'll find you a blanket." He followed her up the narrow stairs to her flat. It was small and cluttered and she was suddenly aware of everything in it—the dishes in the sink, the books piled on every surface, the crack in the ceiling she'd been meaning to fix for a year. She grabbed a blanket from the cupboard and tossed it at him. "Bathroom's down the hall. Towels are clean." "Elin." She turned. "I know this is—I know I'm asking for a lot. And you don't owe me anything. But thank you." "Don't thank me yet," she said. "Thank me when you're gone and everything's the same as it was before." "Is that what you want? Everything the same?" She didn't answer. She went to her bedroom and closed the door. She lay awake, listening. She heard him moving around the living room. She heard the sofa creak as he sat down. She heard the click of a lamp being turned off. Hours passed. She didn't sleep. Around three in the morning, she got up for water. He was lying on the sofa, the blanket pulled up to his chin. His eyes were open. "Can't sleep either?" she said. "Never can. Not in strange places." "This is my faultr." "It's not. It's my fault for being famous and stupid." He shifted, propping himself up on his elbow. "Can I ask you something?" "You're going to anyway." "Why did you let me stay?" She stood in the doorway, holding her glass of water. She could give him a dozen answers. Because it was raining. Because he looked tired. Because she had a spare sofa and it was the decent thing to do. "I don't know," she said. It was the truest thing she'd said all day. He nodded slowly, like he understood. "That's the best answer you could have given me." She went back to bed. This time, she slept. In the morning, he was still there.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 of this story demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on the ambitious plan laid out in the writing intention. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Strengths:** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange between Elin and Dafydd is economical and precise. "Your job is taking pictures of people who don't want to be photographed?" / "Right now, my job is paying my daughter's school fees." This lands exactly as the character profile intended—Dafydd is not a villain, he's a tired man, and the flatness of his delivery ("without self-pity") is well-handled. The chapter avoids making him a convenient antagonist. The late-night conversation is also well-executed. "Can't sleep either?" / "Never can. Not in strange places." / "This is my fault." / "It's not. It's my fault for being famous and stupid." The rhythm here is natural, the self-deprecation feels earned rather than performed, and the exchange has the quality of two people talking in the dark who are slightly more honest than they'd be in daylight. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The plan called for: Dafydd's visit, Rhys arriving panicked, asking to stay, the flat scene with Elin embarrassed by the dishes, the "why haven't you told him" exchange (here reframed as "why did you let me stay"), and the morning reveal that he's still there. All present and accounted for. Character consistency is strong. Rhys cycles through options, wants to flee, but doesn't—consistent with his profile's note that he's reckless and avoidant but also drawn to Elin in a way that disrupts his patterns. Elin's "Don't thank me yet. Thank me when you're gone and everything's the same as it was before" is exactly her voice—dry, self-protective, with the sting of someone who already suspects things won't be the same. The physical specificity the plan called for is present in small doses: the dishes in the sink, the crack in the ceiling, the sofa creak, the lamp click. These are grounding details without being overwrought. **Weaknesses:** There's a continuity error: "This is my faultr." — this appears to be a typo ("faultr" instead of "fault") that slipped through, and it's a minor but real blemish. The chapter plan specified that Rhys should ask "Why haven't you told him I'm here?" and Elin should answer "Because you asked me not to" / "That's not a good reason" / "I know." This exchange, which was meant to be a key moment of honesty, is replaced by the "why did you let me stay" conversation. The replacement is actually good—arguably better—but it does mean the plan's specific emotional beat (Elin acknowledging she's acting against her own interests without a good reason) is somewhat softened. The "I don't know" answer is honest but slightly less pointed than the planned exchange. The line "She thought about the way he'd tucked her hair behind her ear on the pier" is a small problem. This moment hasn't been shown in Chapter 4, and while it presumably happened in Chapter 3, it reads as slightly sentimental—the kind of romantic shorthand that the writing plan explicitly warned against. It's the one moment where the chapter reaches for easy emotional resonance rather than earning it. The opening paragraph—"Some days he bought a book. Some days he just sat in the armchair by the window and read, and she worked around him as if he were part of the furniture. He didn't try to charm her. He didn't fill the silence. He just stayed."—is good compression of the week's passage, but "as if he were part of the furniture" is a slightly worn simile. Not egregious, but not the level of specificity the plan aimed for. The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth. The plan called for "failures and backslides" and "ragged" growth. This chapter moves fairly cleanly from tension (Dafydd's visit) to resolution (Rhys staying, Elin sleeping). The emotional beats are well-placed but not particularly surprising. The "I don't know" answer to "why did you let me stay" is honest but also the most expected honest answer. The plan noted Elin's embarrassment about the flat—"she's embarrassed by the dishes in the sink"—and the chapter includes this, but Rhys "doesn't notice," which is a nice character beat. However, the plan also specified "He paces. She makes tea." The tea-making is absent, which is a small loss—it's a very Elin gesture, the practical comfort-making. **Overall Assessment:** This is a competent, often quite good chapter that executes its plan faithfully and maintains strong character voices. The dialogue is the standout element. The weaknesses are mostly ones of missed opportunity rather than active failure—a slightly too-smooth emotional arc, one sentimental shorthand, and the absence of the planned tea-making scene. The chapter doesn't quite reach the level of the best literary fiction it's aspiring to, but it's genuinely accomplished work that serves the larger story well. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: The Morning She woke to the smell of tea. For a moment, she forgot where she was. The light was wrong—grey and soft through the curtains, not the sharp morning light of her bedroom. Then she remembered. The sofa. The photographer. The man in her living room. She pulled on her dressing gown and walked out. He was standing in her kitchen, wearing yesterday's shirt, holding a steaming mug. He'd found the tea bags. He'd found the kettle. He was reading the spine of a book on her shelf—*The Lonely Londoners*, dog-eared and faded. "That's not what I'd have picked for you," she said. He turned. He looked rumpled and tired and surprisingly unguarded. "What would you have picked?" "Something quieter." He put the book down. "Morning." "Morning." She took the mug he offered her. It was perfect. Exactly how she liked it. She didn't ask how he'd known. "I found the toast," he said. "I burned it." "Good. I like it burned." He laughed. It was small and still half-asleep, and she realised she'd never heard him laugh like that before—without effort, without performance. Just a sound. They sat at her small table. The toast was indeed burned, and she ate it anyway. He watched her. "What?" she said. "Nothing. I'm just—" He shook his head. "I don't know what I'm doing here." "In Llanfawr?" "Here. In your flat. Drinking your tea. Eating your burned toast." He paused. "I don't know why I'm still here. I should have left hours ago." "Why haven't you?" He looked at her. "Because I don't want to." She didn't know what to say to that. She took another bite of toast. He told her about his childhood. It came out in fragments, between sips of tea, like he was testing how much he could say without being judged. His mother worked three jobs. His father left when he was seven—not dramatically, just didn't come home one day. He started acting because he discovered that being someone else was easier than being himself. He was good at it. Too good. "I still don't know who I am when no one's watching," he said. She considered this. "I know who I am when no one's watching." "Who?" "Someone who reads too much and talks to her cat and doesn't know how to let people in." "Is that who you really are, or who you've told yourself you are?" She blinked. The question landed somewhere deep. She didn't answer. She reached across the table and took his hand. She didn't plan it. Her hand just moved, and then it was there, resting on top of his. He looked down at it like he'd never seen anything like it. "You don't have to say anything," she said. "You don't have to perform. You can just sit here and be quiet and drink your tea, and I won't ask for anything else." He turned his hand over and laced his fingers through hers. They sat like that for a long time. The tea went cold. The toast grew stiff on the plate. Outside, the rain started again, tapping against the window. "I had a dream about you," he said. She didn't pull her hand away. "What kind of dream?" "The kind where we were walking somewhere. I don't know where. And you were laughing. I don't think I've ever heard you laugh." "I laugh." "Not like that. Not the way you do when you forget yourself." She looked at their hands, joined on the table. "Maybe I haven't had anything to laugh about." "Is that my fault?" "No. But maybe you're part of the solution." He squeezed her hand. "I don't know how to be part of a solution. I've spent my whole life being part of the problem." "Then learn." He looked at her. His eyes were wet. She pretended not to notice. "I should go," he said. "Before Dafydd wakes up and follows me back here." "You said you'd leave this morning." "I know." "Is that still the plan?" He was quiet for a long moment. She could see him wrestling with something. Finally, he said, "I'm not leaving. I mean—I'm leaving the flat. But I'm not leaving Llanfawr. Not yet." "How long?" "I don't know. A week. Two weeks." He met her eyes. "As long as you'll let me stay." "You can't stay forever." "I know." "And I can't be the reason you stay." "You're not the reason." He paused. "You're the reason I want to. But if I stay, it has to be my choice." She nodded slowly. "Then stay. For now." He stood up. He gathered his coat from the back of the chair. At the door, he turned. "Elin." "What?" "I read the poems because of you. I came back because of you. I'm still here because of you." He paused. "I don't know what I'm trying to say." "Yes, you do." He looked at her for a long moment. Then he opened the door and left. She stood in her kitchen, surrounded by the remains of a breakfast she hadn't planned. The tea was cold. The toast was stiff. Her hand was still warm from where his had been. She picked up his mug and held it for a long time before she washed it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a quiet, intimate chapter that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: render a morning-after scene (chaste, but charged) between two people who are circling something neither can name. The writing is controlled and mostly earns its restraint. Let me examine it carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter opens well. "She woke to the smell of tea" is simple and effective, grounding us immediately in sensory reality without overwriting. The detail of him having found the tea bags, the kettle, and making it exactly how she likes it without being asked is a small, precise piece of characterization that shows rather than tells. The burned toast exchange is genuinely good dialogue—economical, revealing, with a light touch of humor that doesn't strain. The chapter follows its plan closely. The plan called for: him reading The Lonely Londoners, the "something quieter" exchange, the childhood backstory in fragments, the "I know who I am when no one's watching" reversal, the hand-holding, the cold toast, and the departure with "I'm not leaving Llanfawr. Not yet." All of these are present and executed with reasonable fidelity. The hand-holding scene is handled well. "She didn't plan it. Her hand just moved, and then it was there" avoids sentimentality while conveying the involuntary nature of the gesture. His response—looking at it "like he'd never seen anything like it"—is a strong beat. The dialogue is generally in voice. Rhys's line "I've spent my whole life being part of the problem" is consistent with his character profile. Elin's "Then learn" is exactly the kind of short, dry response the profile calls for. **Weaknesses:** The chapter plan specified that Elin reaches across the table and takes his hand—but in the plan, it's Rhys who reaches across. The reversal (making it Elin who initiates) is actually a more interesting choice and consistent with her arc, so this is a productive deviation. However, the plan also specified "Neither of them speaks. The toast goes cold." The chapter adds considerable dialogue after the hand-holding, which dilutes the silence the plan was aiming for. The plan's version—sitting in silence, the toast going cold—would have been more powerful than what we get, which is more dialogue and explanation. The dream sequence is the chapter's weakest moment. "I had a dream about you... where we were walking somewhere... and you were laughing" is a fairly generic romantic device. The plan didn't include this, and it doesn't add much. It's the kind of thing that sounds meaningful but is actually vague and slightly clichéd. The exchange "Is that who you really are, or who you've told yourself you are?" is a sharp question, but Elin's non-answer ("She didn't answer") is a slight cheat—the plan called for her to say "I know who I am when no one's watching. It's when people are watching that I forget," which is a much better line and was apparently dropped or rearranged. The chapter does include a version of this ("Someone who reads too much and talks to her cat and doesn't know how to let people in") but it's less resonant than the planned line. Rhys's final speech at the door—"I read the poems because of you. I came back because of you. I'm still here because of you"—is the chapter's most problematic moment. It's too explicit, too declarative, and slightly out of character for a man who the profile says is "incapable of sitting in silence" and fills space with jokes rather than confessions. The plan called for him to say "I'm not leaving. I mean—I'm leaving the flat, but I'm not leaving Llanfawr. Not yet." That's the right register. The additional speech tips into the kind of emotional politeness the writing plan explicitly warned against. The ending—"She picked up his mug and held it for a long time before she washed it"—is good. It's specific, physical, and earns its sentiment without stating it. The chapter is slightly too tidy. The plan warned against "emotional politeness" and "characters say things that land wrong." Here, things land mostly right, which is actually a problem—the ragged quality the plan called for is somewhat smoothed over. The "his eyes were wet. She pretended not to notice" moment is fine but slightly predictable. **Dialogue assessment:** The dialogue is mostly strong but occasionally slips into the expository. "I've spent my whole life being part of the problem" and "I don't know how to be part of a solution" are slightly on-the-nose. The best dialogue in the chapter is the early exchanges about toast and tea—short, oblique, revealing. **Overall:** A competent, often affecting chapter that follows its plan well but occasionally softens the edges the plan called for. The writing is clean and controlled, with genuine moments of specificity and restraint. The weaknesses are real but not fatal. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: The Photo The photo appeared on a Wednesday afternoon. Elin was shelving returns when her phone buzzed. A text from her brother in Australia: *Is that you?* Attached was a screenshot. A grainy image, taken from a distance, through a window. Two figures standing close. A man and a woman. The woman was her. She zoomed in. The angle was wrong, the light was bad, but there was no mistaking it. Her face, half-turned. Rhys's hand on her shoulder. The window of her flat. Her stomach dropped. She checked the article. It was already online. *Calamity Caradog's Countryside Retreat.* The text was short and cruel. "The scandal-plagued actor has been hiding out in a small Welsh town with a mystery woman—a local bookshop owner who appears to have become his new confidante." They'd used a photo from his red carpet days next to the one of her, the contrast designed to make her look plain, provincial, irrelevant. She put her phone face-down on the counter. Customers came and went. She served them. She took their money. She said thank you and have a nice day. She was operating on muscle memory, her mind elsewhere. Angharad texted: *I saw. Are you okay?* She didn't answer. Bronwen jumped onto the counter and meowed. Elin stroked her automatically. "It's fine," she said aloud. "It's just a photo. It doesn't mean anything." But it did. It meant her quiet life was no longer quiet. It meant people would look at her differently now, would whisper when she walked past. It meant her mother would call. Her mother called. "Is that you in the picture?" "Yes." "Is that the actor?" "Yes." "Elin, what are you doing?" "I don't know." Her mother was quiet for a moment. "Be careful," she said. "That's all I'm saying." She hung up. The shop door opened. Rhys walked in. He looked terrible—dark circles, unwashed hair, a desperation in his eyes she hadn't seen before. "You've seen it," he said. "Yes." "I'm so sorry. I shouldn't have stayed. I should have left when I said I would." "You can't control what photographers do." "I could have controlled whether I was here to be photographed." He ran his hands through his hair. "I'll leave. I'll go to Prague tonight. I'll tell them I'm not making a statement, I'll refuse to answer questions, I'll—" "Stop." He stopped. "Sit down." He looked at her, confused, but he sat. He perched on the armchair by the window, his hands gripping his knees. She walked over and stood in front of him. "You don't get to decide this alone." "What?" "You don't get to decide what happens next. You don't get to run away because you feel guilty. I get a say." "Elin, I've already ruined—" "You haven't ruined anything." She crouched down so she was at eye level with him. "They took a photo. They made up a story. That's what they do. It doesn't change anything between us." "How can you say that? Your face is all over the internet. Your customers are going to look at you differently. Your mother—" "My mother called. She told me to be careful. That's all." He stared at her. "You're not angry?" "I'm angry that they took a picture of me without my permission. I'm angry that they called me a 'mystery woman' like I'm a character in their story. But I'm not angry at you." "Why not?" She didn't have an answer. She was angry. Some part of her was furious—at him, at Dafydd, at herself for letting this happen. But looking at him now, seeing how broken he was, she couldn't hold onto it. "I don't know," she said. "Maybe I should be. But I'm not." He reached out and took her hand. "I don't deserve you." "That's not for you to decide." He almost smiled. "You keep telling me what's not for me to decide." "Someone has to." She stood up. She pulled him to his feet. He didn't let go of her hand. "Are you staying?" she asked. "I said I'd leave." "I know what you said. I'm asking you what you want." He looked at her. She could see him fighting with himself—the instinct to flee, to protect her by disappearing, warring with something else. Something that wanted to stay. "What do you want?" he asked. "I asked you first." "I want to stay. But I'm scared that staying will hurt you." "Let me decide what hurts me." He looked at her for a long moment. Then he nodded. "I'll stay." She squeezed his hand. "Good." They stood there, in the middle of the shop, holding hands. The bell didn't jangle. No one came in. For a moment, the world outside—the photographers, the articles, the whispers—didn't exist. "I meant what I said," she said. "I forgive you. But if you run now, I won't forgive you for that." "I won't run." "Promise?" He met her eyes. "I promise." She believed him. She didn't know if she should. But she believed him. Bronwen meowed from the counter. Rhys laughed—a real laugh, surprised out of him. "Does she always do that?" "Only when she wants attention." "Sounds familiar." Elin smiled. It was small, and it didn't last long, but it was real.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a competent but somewhat disappointing execution of what the plan promised. Let me work through the key issues and strengths. **Deviation from the Chapter Plan** The plan for Chapter 6 was explicit: the argument should be "ugly." Rhys says something sharp about having his life dissected; Elin fires back about her quiet life being set on fire; he says "I'm sorry" and she says "that's not enough." He leaves. The bell jangles. She stands in the empty shop. She does not cry. The chapter was supposed to end in rupture, with him leaving, setting up the five-day separation in Chapter 7. What the model delivered is almost the opposite: a reconciliation scene. Elin is forgiving, warm, even tender. She crouches to his eye level, holds his hand, says "I forgive you," and they end the chapter smiling at the cat together. This is a fundamental betrayal of the plan, which explicitly called for "ugly" conflict and a departure. The plan's emotional logic—that the rupture earns the return in Chapter 7—is undermined entirely. The kiss in Chapter 7 was supposed to happen "during an argument, not after a confession," and was described as "desperate and awkward and real." By resolving the tension here, the model has flattened the arc. **Dialogue Problems** The dialogue is functional but frequently falls into the trap the plan warned against: "emotional politeness." Characters say things that are too clean, too resolved. "You don't get to decide what happens next" is a reasonable line, but it's followed by a string of exchanges that feel like a therapy session rather than two damaged people talking past each other. The plan noted that characters should "say things that land wrong"—almost nothing lands wrong here. Rhys's line "I don't deserve you" is a cliché that the plan's character profile would never allow—Rhys is a performer who uses language precisely, and this is the most generic thing a man can say in this situation. Elin's response ("That's not for you to decide") is sharp, but it's the only sharp thing in a chapter that should be full of edges. The plan specified Rhys "overuses people's names" as a vocal tic. He doesn't use her name once in this chapter. Elin's habit of "swallowing her words when she's nervous" is absent. The idiosyncrasies that make these characters specific have been smoothed away. **Tell-Don't-Show** "He looked terrible—dark circles, unwashed hair, a desperation in his eyes she hadn't seen before." This is telling. The plan called for physical specificity—"the weight of a book in the hand, the sound of the bell, the smell of rain on wool." The chapter opens with a phone buzzing and a screenshot, which is fine, but the emotional beats are frequently stated rather than embodied. "She was operating on muscle memory, her mind elsewhere" is a tell. "She could see him fighting with himself" is a tell. **What Works** The opening sequence—the text from her brother, the article, the customers coming and going, the mother's call—is economical and effective. The mother's dialogue ("Be careful. That's all I'm saying.") is exactly right: understated, loaded, specific to a Welsh mother's register. Bronwen the cat's appearance at the end is a nice touch, and Rhys's laugh being "surprised out of him" echoes Chapter 2's language in a way that feels intentional and earned. The structure of the phone calls (brother, Angharad, mother) efficiently conveys the ripple effect of the photo without belaboring it. The detail about the article using a red carpet photo next to the grainy one "to make her look plain, provincial, irrelevant" is sharp and specific. **Character Consistency** Elin is significantly out of character. The plan describes her as avoidant—"when things get hard, she goes quiet and waits for them to pass." Here she is proactive, emotionally articulate, and forgiving in a way that contradicts her profile. Her pride, described as "fear of rejection," would not allow her to be this openly warm this quickly. The plan's Elin would have gone cold, not warm. Rhys is somewhat more consistent—his guilt, his impulse to flee, his self-flagellation—but the "I don't deserve you" line is beneath the character as written. **Tone** The plan called for "failures and backslides" and warned against "clean redemptive arcs." This chapter is a clean redemptive arc compressed into 1,000 words. The ending—holding hands, smiling at the cat—is the kind of emotional resolution the plan explicitly said to avoid. The chapter ends with warmth where it should end with absence. **Overall Assessment** This is competent, readable prose that fails its own ambitions. The writing is clean, the pacing is reasonable, and there are individual moments of genuine quality. But the chapter fundamentally misreads its own plan, delivers a reconciliation where a rupture was promised, and smooths away the roughness that would have made these characters feel real. It's the kind of chapter that would satisfy a reader who wants comfort but disappoint one who wants truth. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 9 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 8 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 9 Tell-Don't-Show: 10 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 12 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 7 Characters Consistent with Profile: 7 Followed Chapter Plan: 4 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 12

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: The Distance He stayed. But something had shifted. The photo was out there. People knew. He couldn't walk to the shop without being seen, couldn't buy milk without someone taking a picture on their phone. The B&B owner gave him a sympathetic look when he came down for breakfast. The story had spread. He started leaving earlier, arriving later. He stopped coming to the shop during opening hours. He'd text her after closing, a single word: *Open?* She always replied: *Yes.* They'd sit in the back room, away from the windows, and talk about nothing. He was quieter now. The charm had evaporated entirely, replaced by something raw and uncertain. He didn't try to make her laugh. He didn't fill the silence. He just sat with her, and she sat with him, and that was enough. But it wasn't enough for him. "I'm no good at this," he said one evening. They were sitting on the floor of the stockroom, surrounded by boxes of unsold books. "Sitting still. Waiting. Not knowing what comes next." "What comes next is we get up tomorrow and do the same thing." "I don't know if I can." "Then leave." He flinched. "That's not what I meant." "It's what you said." "I meant—I don't know how to live like this. Small. Quiet. Not performing." She looked at him. He was staring at his hands. She saw the restlessness in him, the way his leg bounced, the way his eyes darted around the room. He was a man who needed a stage, an audience, a role to play. And she was asking him to just be. "Maybe that's why you should stay," she said. He looked up. "Maybe you need to learn how to be small. How to be quiet. How to exist without someone watching." "And what if I can't?" "Then you'll leave. And I'll understand." He reached for her hand. She let him take it. "I don't want to leave," he said. "Then don't." "It's not that simple." "It is." She squeezed his fingers. "You just keep deciding not to. Every day. One day at a time." He looked at her for a long moment. "You make it sound easy." "It's not easy. It's simple. There's a difference." He nodded slowly. "When did you get so wise?" "Living in a small town. Reading too many books. Talking to my cat." He laughed. It was barely a sound, more an exhale than anything, but it was real. She felt something loosen in her chest. That night, after he left, she lay awake thinking about him. About the way he'd looked at her in the stockroom, like she was something he didn't understand and wanted to. About the way his hand had felt in hers. She was in trouble. She knew she was in trouble. She had spent three years building a quiet life, a small life, a life where no one could hurt her. And he had walked in with his beautiful face and his broken heart and his desperate need to be seen, and she had let him. She had let him in. She didn't know if she could let him go. --- Five days passed. Then seven. He came to the shop less often. He was making calls, making plans. She could feel him pulling away, preparing himself to leave. She didn't ask. She didn't push. She let him come to her when he was ready. He came on a Thursday, just before closing. He looked worse than before—thinner, darker circles, a tightness around his mouth that hadn't been there. "I've been thinking," he said. "About what?" "About what you said. About deciding every day." He paused. "I've been deciding every day to stay. But I don't know if that's enough." "It's all there is." "I know." He stepped closer. "But I also know that I'm going to have to leave. The film starts in three weeks. I have to be in Prague." "I know." "I don't want to leave you." "Then don't." "Elin—" "I'm not going to make this easy for you." Her voice was steady, but her hands were shaking. "If you want to leave, leave. But don't ask me to tell you it's okay. It's not okay. It's going to hurt. And I'm going to let it hurt because that's what it means to care about someone." He stared at her. "You care about me?" "You know I do." "I didn't know. I wasn't sure." She stepped forward and kissed him. It wasn't a good kiss. It was awkward and desperate and their teeth clicked. She pulled back and looked at him. "That's your answer." He touched his lips. "I don't know if this is a mistake." "Neither do I." They stood in the middle of the shop, close but not touching. The bell did not ring. No one came in. "I don't know how to do this," he said. "I don't know how to be with someone without performing. I don't know how to be honest when I've spent my whole life being someone else." "Then learn." "With you?" "If you want to." He looked at her. His eyes were wet. "I want to." She took his hand. "Then start now." He didn't say anything. He just held her hand and stood with her in the quiet of the shop, as the light faded outside and the world kept turning without them.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a pivotal chapter in the story's arc, and it's worth examining carefully against both the writing plan and the broader prompt requirements. **Adherence to Plan:** The plan specified that Chapter 7 would cover five days of distance, Rhys staying at a B&B, Mrs. Davies asking about the photo, and then—crucially—the kiss happening "during an argument, not after a confession," described as "awkward and desperate and real," with teeth clicking. The chapter does deliver the kiss with teeth clicking and the awkwardness, but the plan's specific instruction that it happen "during an argument" is only partially honored. There is a tense exchange, but it reads more like an emotional confrontation than a genuine argument. The plan also specified Mrs. Davies asking about the man in the photo, which is absent. The stockroom scene is an invention not in the plan, which is fine, but it somewhat dilutes the five-day distance the plan called for. The chapter compresses and rearranges the plan's beats in ways that occasionally work but sometimes feel rushed. **Dialogue Quality:** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element in places and weakest in others. Lines like "It's not easy. It's simple. There's a difference" are genuinely good—they feel like Elin, they carry weight without overexplaining. The exchange about "deciding every day" has real texture. However, there are several moments where the dialogue becomes too on-the-nose and explanatory. "I don't know how to be with someone without performing. I don't know how to be honest when I've spent my whole life being someone else" is Rhys essentially narrating his own character profile at the reader. This is a significant flaw—it tells us what we should be inferring from behavior. Similarly, "I don't want to leave you" / "Then don't" / "It's not that simple" / "It is" is a pattern that repeats too often in the chapter, becoming a verbal tic of the writing rather than a character tic. **Show vs. Tell:** The chapter has a tell-don't-show problem in its prose sections. "The charm had evaporated entirely, replaced by something raw and uncertain" is pure telling. "She was in trouble. She knew she was in trouble" is the narrator explaining Elin's emotional state rather than dramatizing it. The plan specifically called for internal states to be "shown through behavior, never named." The chapter violates this repeatedly. The stockroom scene has some physical specificity (sitting on the floor, boxes of unsold books, bouncing leg, darting eyes) but then undercuts it with explicit emotional narration. **Character Consistency:** Rhys's vocal tic of repeating names is absent here, which is appropriate given the intimacy of the stage, but his tendency to fill silence has been replaced by a quietness that feels slightly inconsistent with his profile—though the plan does note he becomes quieter after the photo incident. Elin's avoidance and pride are present, but her declaration "It's going to hurt. And I'm going to let it hurt because that's what it means to care about someone" feels slightly too articulate and self-aware for a character described as someone who "swallows her words when she's nervous" and is avoidant.
